SESSION COMPLETE

CHARISMA COMPANY: FIZZLEBLAST’S FIELDTRIP

A Charisma Company One-Shot

ONE-SHOT // THE CHULTAN EXPEDITION // ~3H
🛡️
Moor
Cleric • Kyle
📜
Aramil
Warlock • Kyle
🪓
Opal
Barbarian • Shannon
🔮
Lavender
Warlock • Kelsey
🗡️
Bob
Rogue • Josh
Session Recap

Story So Far

The Eye and the Everlasting Dark

The golden serpent hung from the archway like an offering to something older than prayer, its jeweled eyes catching the last light of a dying sun — and one hand, quick and faithless, reached for what should never have been taken.

Forbidden Shores

The canoes scraped against black sand, and the jungle screamed.

Not figuratively. The wall of green ahead shrieked with a thousand unseen throats — howler monkeys, cicadas sawing their legs raw, things with too many limbs rustling through canopies so dense they swallowed the sky. The air hit them like a wet cloth pressed against the mouth. It did not carry humidity so much as weight, thick with the smell of rot and scale and earth turned over by massive, unseen feet.

Five strangers climbed out of those canoes. They had answered the same flyer — posted by a gnome named Fizzleblast in a forum that catered equally to mercenaries, murderers, and mothers-in-law — and each of them had their own reason for being stupid enough to come to Chult.

Moor stepped onto the shore first, his white robes already spotted with river spray. A dragonborn cleric of Tyr, he carried himself with the economy of someone who'd learned long ago that words were a luxury. His shield caught the sun. His eyes, amber and reptilian, swept the treeline without blinking.

Opal followed, and the canoe rocked upward with the sudden absence of her weight. She was goliath through and through — hewn from the same stone as mountain faces, arms like quarry blocks, and a smile that suggested she found the entire concept of danger genuinely delightful. She sniffed the air with enormous enthusiasm. "Do we smell dinosaurs?"

Behind her, a half-elf in a stained fedora picked his way across the gunwale with the careful precision of a man who expected the world to betray him at every step. Aramil looked like an adventurer who had peaked a decade ago and spent the intervening years trying to convince himself otherwise. A purple crystal hung against his chest. His eyes darted everywhere.

Lavender was the color her name promised — jewels glittering along the curve of her horns, her tiefling face bright with an optimism that seemed medically implausible given the circumstances. A locket hung at her throat, and sometimes the wind around it moved in ways the breeze couldn't explain. Her imp, Tika, perched on her shoulder in the shape of a raven, one beady eye scanning the jungle with the weary professionalism of a bodyguard who'd seen better postings.

And then there was Bob. Very short. Backpack bristling with gear. Hood so deep his face was nothing but the glint of eyes and, occasionally, the suggestion of a mustache. When asked to describe himself, he looked at the back of his hand where something was apparently written. "I'm Bob."

"Just Bob?" Fizzleblast called from the bow of his canoe, where he stood in a cashmere robe three sizes too large, spectacles the width of dinner plates threatening to slide off his nose. "Your parents weren't out of room on the birth certificate?"

"Bob."

Fizzleblast's intern, Niles — a red-haired boy still waging war with puberty — struggled to drag an enormous sack from the second canoe. The gnome paid him no attention, instead launching into a briefing delivered at the speed and coherence of a man who had recently consumed his body weight in coffee. The gist: somewhere inside the temple ahead, there was a golden, snake-shaped artifact. Fizzleblast wanted it. Everything else was theirs to keep. A rival team was an hour behind them. The canoe rental expired at noon tomorrow. And whatever they did, they should absolutely not press the --

Fizzleblast stepped backward into his protective bubble and vanished. The sound cut off mid-sentence.

"Do not press the button," Lavender said, very slowly, to no one.

Niles, staggering up the beach under the weight of the loot sack, stepped on a crab. His feet went out from under him, and the bag — a bag of holding stuffed well past capacity — ripped open and flung its contents across the sand like a geyser of enchanted debris. Potions arced through the air. Scrolls unfurled. And a vial of something Fizzleblast had screamed about — a potion of instant attunement — shattered on the ground in front of them, spattering them all with binding magic that married each adventurer to whatever landed on them.

Moor found himself suddenly encased in adamantine plate armor, its surface drinking light like oil. Opal caught a pearl that pulsed with stored power. Lavender's forearms hummed beneath bracers that bent light around them. Aramil flexed his fingers inside gloves so fine they seemed woven from shadow itself — gloves made for thieves.

And Bob, somewhere in the chaos, acquired a pair of slippers that stuck to surfaces. He pressed his palm flat against the canoe hull and felt it grip like a gecko's foot.

"Consider it a signing bonus," Fizzleblast's voice drifted from behind the shimmering safety field. "Now get out of here."

The Antechamber of Blades

The temple entrance gaped before them, flanked by heliographic thermal arrays — mirrors angled to focus sunlight into beams that could melt flesh from bone. Two gorallons, four-armed jungle apes with brows like cliff shelves, paced at the edge of the light, snarling. Their skin blistered where the beams caught them, but hunger kept them circling.

Moor walked to the nearest mirror, gripped its stone housing, and wrenched. The mechanism groaned. The beam swung like a lighthouse sweep and caught the first gorallon full in the chest. The creature lunged — one final, desperate leap — and the concentrated light punched through it. The smell was cooked meat and burnt hair. It collapsed into steaming ruin at Moor's feet.

The second gorallon watched its companion dissolve. It was not intelligent, but it possessed a survival instinct sharpened by millennia of jungle predation. It hesitated.

Aramil stepped forward. He spoke to it in its own tongue — a gift of his pact that let him converse with beasts. "Hey, big guy," he said, his voice carrying the reasonable cadence of a professor explaining why a student should withdraw from the course. "You just saw your friend get blasted, right? You want that? Think about your wife and kids."

The gorallon's demeanor collapsed. It shrieked, turned, and bolted into the jungle.

"You're welcome," Aramil said to no one in particular.

They pressed inside. The antechamber was rectangular and thirty feet high, the ceiling lost in shadow and cobwebs. The air tasted of old copper and dry blood. Along the walls, heaped to eye level, lay treasure — gold coins in staggering quantities, weapons, trinkets, and the occasional magical artifact half-buried in the glitter. Interspersed among the riches lay skeletons in various stages of decay, grinning at the ceiling.

The floor told the real story. Two rows of pressure plates ran the length of the chamber, and the residue of old violence — dried blood in arterial sprays — marked where previous adventurers had learned their function. Moor extended his mace and pressed the nearest plate. A blade erupted from a slit in the wall with the sound of a sword being drawn at speed, close enough to part the air beside his arm. The entire floor section tilted, dumping its contents sideways.

"Noted," Moor said.

At the far end, a mural depicted a man and a crocodile walking in lockstep, arms linked, swords crossed overhead. It was the key to everything. Moor studied it, then turned to Lavender. "You and I. Side by side. Step for step."

They crossed together, pressing each pair of plates in synchronous rhythm while the others watched. One step. Two. The blades stayed sheathed. At the far wall, two rusted greatswords hung above the main gate. They each gripped one and pulled. Gears groaned somewhere deep in the stone, and the massive portcullis ground upward.

"Run through door," Moor called to the others. "Why use many word when small word do trick?"

Bob scurried along the ceiling, bypassing the pressure plates entirely. Aramil and Bob sprinted across together, their footfalls matching by instinct or luck. The party tumbled through the gate, and the stone crashed down behind them.

The Crystalline Heart

The prism room was the temple's mechanical soul — a cathedral of redirected light and ancient engineering. A massive crystalline prism hung from a bar in the center of the vaulted space, splitting the beams that fed it into spectral lances. Two enormous stone snake statues flanked the room, their hollow eyes serving as reflectors. Four unlit gems crowned the far portcullis. The message was clear: light all four, and the way opens.

But the room had teeth.

Opal found that out first. She gripped one of the floor-mounted mirrors and heaved. Her fingers slipped. She toppled forward directly into the focused beam. Light seared across her face like a brand. She screamed — not from pain, though the pain was considerable — but from the sudden, absolute absence of sight. The world went white, then black, then nothing.

"I'm blind," she announced, with the matter-of-fact tone of someone reporting the weather. Blood ran from her scorched eyes. Fourteen points of damage and her vision, gone.

Moor was beside her in moments. His medicine told him it would pass — an hour, maybe less, or any healing magic would mend what the light had burned. But his second-level spell was precious, and they were barely past the threshold. He pressed a bandana into her hands.

Lavender knelt beside the goliath and tied a silk scarf gently across Opal's ruined eyes. "There," she said softly. "Very fashionable."

"I am Goliath," Opal said. "I am Opal. I am fine."

Lavender took her hand.

Then the dead monkey fell.

A feral baboon, half-melted by the mirror arrays above, crashed through the upper terrace and landed on a pressure plate. Noxious gas erupted from vents along the floor — thick, green, and spreading fast. There was no outrunning it. The cloud swallowed them.

Every one of them choked. Lungs seized. Eyes watered. The poison settled into muscle and bone, turning strength to water. Fourteen points of damage ripped through the party in a single toxic breath.

"Eleven hit points," Opal reported, blind and poisoned.

"Twelve," Aramil wheezed.

Moor gripped his staff of healing — newly found in the treasure piles — and poured its power outward. White light rippled through the gas like dawn through fog. It would not be the last time the dragonborn held his companions together with borrowed magic and sheer stubbornness.

Lavender raised her hands and spoke a word. Wind erupted from her palms — sixty feet of gale force that scattered the gas like smoke from a candle. She stood in the eye of it, horns tilted forward, and did not flinch.

Then the snake came.

Blood in the Prism Light

It emerged from the mouth of one of the stone serpent statues, pouring out like oil — a constrictor of impossible length, forty feet from blunt nose to tapered tail, its body thick as a man's torso. Heat had roused it from whatever ancient slumber the temple's climate had imposed. It tasted the air with a tongue like a black ribbon and turned its flat head toward the warmest bodies in the room.

Aramil tried to reason with it. He spoke in the beast-tongue, his voice carrying the cadence of academic authority. "Brother Snake. There are tasty monkeys up top. Much easier prey."

The snake was unimpressed.

"I chop the snake," Opal said. She could not see it. She could hear its scales whispering across stone, feel the vibration of its mass through her bare feet, and that was enough. She swung her greataxe in a wide arc. It whistled past the serpent's head — the creature faster than its bulk suggested.

Lavender sent Tika diving. The imp, still in raven form, drove its beak into the snake's left eye. The creature reared, hissing, and Aramil's blast of eldritch force caught it in the throat. Bob's arrow punched through a coil from the shadows, sneak attack damage doubled by Opal's proximity.

But the snake was ancient and furious. It struck at the raven and caught Tika in its jaws — venom and crushing force together. The imp shuddered, went rigid, and dissolved into the ethereal mist that claimed fey creatures when their borrowed forms failed.

Then it turned on Aramil.

The half-elf never saw it coming. The constrictor lunged past Opal's guard and wrapped itself around Aramil's torso with the speed of a snapping cable. Coil after coil tightened. Ribs compressed. Aramil's mouth opened in a soundless gasp as the air was crushed from him. Twenty-two points of damage in a single constriction — his consciousness flickered, guttered, and went out. He hung limp in the serpent's embrace, blood on his lips.

"He's down," Moor said, already moving. He pressed his palm to Aramil's chest through the gap between coils and poured healing magic into the warlock's failing body.

Opal heard the snake's muscles flex. She heard the wet rasp of scales shifting. She raised her greataxe overhead — blind, poisoned, running on instinct and fury — and brought it down.

The blade caught the serpent where its coils layered thickest. It sheared through one loop, then another, the enchanted edge of her bloodaxe biting deep. The snake's body convulsed. Its grip on Aramil slackened. One more stroke, and the creature split apart in a wash of dark ichor, its halves sliding away from the unconscious warlock like shed skin.

"That was not my most creative kill," Opal said, breathing hard, blood-soaked bandana still tied across her eyes. "But snakes freak me out."

Moor's cure wounds brought Aramil gasping back to consciousness. The half-elf blinked up at the prism room's ceiling, alive by a margin thinner than the snake's fangs.

The Sleeping King

They climbed.

Through corridors thick with rising heat. Past the turning cranks that redirected the mirror beams. Past treasure hoards they did not have time to properly loot, though they grabbed what they could — a helm of telepathy for Aramil, an alchemy jug and a manual of quickness for Moor, a bloodaxe and periapt of wound closure for Opal, boots of striding and springing that would later save them all.

The puzzle of the four gems yielded to brute force and lateral thinking. Lavender, immune to fire by virtue of the Zephyr Reliquary she'd claimed from the temple's cooling system, walked through the beams as casually as crossing a room. She and Opal — her vision restored by Moor's healing — muscled the snake statues into alignment, redirecting the refracted light until all four gems blazed crimson. The final portcullis rose.

Beyond it lay open sky.

They stood at the temple's crown, a natural canyon enclosed by ridged stone walls. The jungle pressed in from above — crocodiles lined the ridgeline, boa constrictors draped over ledges, gorallons watching with hungry, patient eyes. None of them descended. None of them dared.

Because in the center of the canyon, bathing in the last concentrated warmth of the mirror arrays, lay the tyrant king of Chult's food chain. A tyrannosaur. Its flanks rose and fell with the slow rhythm of deep sleep, each exhalation a gust that stirred dust and small bones. Its hide was scarred, its teeth visible even with its jaws closed — each one the length of a dagger.

And above it, hanging from an archway like a temple offering, gleamed the golden snake idol. Its jeweled eyes caught the fading light and threw it back in prismatic fire.

"I'm going," Bob said.

No debate. No committee. The halfling pulled himself up the canyon wall in total darkness, fingers and boots finding holds with the surety of a man born climbing. At the top of the arch, he inverted — hanging by his legs twenty feet above a sleeping predator that could swallow him in a single bite — and reached down.

The idol was heavier than it looked. Solid gold, snake-coiled, warm from the focused sunlight. Bob's gloved fingers closed around it. He lifted, twisted, and slid it into his pack with a sleight of hand so smooth it barely rustled.

Then he let go of the arch, spread his bat-wing cloak, and dropped into the dark air like a stone with wings.

The fireball caught him mid-glide.

The Escape

The world turned orange. A roaring sphere of flame erupted from the ridgeline above — launched by one of the rival adventurers who had been watching, waiting, planning to let someone else do the hard work and steal the prize. The fireball missed Bob by inches, his reflexes bending his glide into a desperate roll. Half the damage. Survivable.

The tyrannosaur was less fortunate. The full blast caught it broadside, scorching ancient hide and shattering the archway above its head. Stone rained down. The beast lurched upright with a roar that shook loose rocks from the canyon walls — a sound like a cathedral collapsing, like the earth itself splitting at the seams.

"Run," someone said. It might have been all of them.

Four minutes and twenty seconds. That was how long they had to reach Fizzleblast's safety bubble before the Chultan jungle — now fully dark, now fully alive with every nocturnal predator and risen corpse the peninsula could produce — consumed them entirely.

The skill challenge unfolded at a dead sprint. Aramil threw himself at the closing inner door with an athletics check that barely cleared the threshold. Lavender vaulted the gas-trap pressure plate with an acrobat's grace. Bob launched himself across a thirty-six-foot gap with the enchanted boots, sailing over blade traps that could not touch him. Opal and Moor, muscles screaming, hauled the counterweight swords upward to lift the portcullis one final time, then dove through as it crashed behind them.

They hit the antechamber running. Two of them stumbled on the pressure plates — the blades erupted, slicing through armor and flesh. Thirteen points of damage each. Two failures on the skill challenge. One more, and someone would die in this hallway, thirty yards from safety.

Bob sailed overhead, propelled by a standing long jump tripled by his enchanted boots. He cleared the entire trapped corridor in a single bound.

The stone door at the entrance was down. Sealed. Five of them threw themselves at the ropes, hauling with everything they had left. The stone groaned, shifted, and rose. They tumbled out into the night.

Two mages stood between them and the bubble. The rival party's last survivors, positioned by dimension door, desperation bright in their eyes.

Aramil's phantasmal force hit one of them — an illusion of raptors pouring from the darkness, teeth and claws and hunger. The mage's nerve broke entirely. He grabbed his companion and fled, dragging him into the jungle.

The party crossed the threshold of Fizzleblast's safety field, and the world beyond it erupted. The dead clawed up from Chultan soil. The jungle predators descended from the ridgelines. The rival adventurers' screams lasted only seconds before they were swallowed by the dark.

Inside the bubble, Fizzleblast slept soundly, clutching a stuffed bear. Niles lay on the floor, shaking, having witnessed something through the translucent barrier that had drained the color from his face permanently.

The party collapsed on the sand, breathing hard, bleeding, alive.

The Stolen Eye

Dawn should have come.

The party slept in shifts within the safety field, and when the hours passed and the sky remained the same flat, starless black it had been at midnight, no one said anything at first. Exhaustion has a way of excusing the impossible. Perhaps they had miscounted. Perhaps the jungle canopy was simply too thick.

But Aramil knew.

He sat apart from the others, turning something over in his fingers — a gem the size of a quail's egg, pried from the socket of the golden snake idol with the gloves of thievery and an eighteen on his sleight of hand check. The Eye of the Serpent. Worth five thousand gold, perhaps more. Worth, potentially, everything.

He had taken it as they loaded the canoes. While Fizzleblast snored. While Niles trembled. While his companions counted their bruises and marveled at their survival. He had slipped his hand into the pack, found the idol's face, and worked the gem free with practiced, treacherous fingers. No one saw.

He thought of the mural in the antechamber. The serpent coiled around the sun disk. The sun's rays bending toward its eye. He thought of the second mural — the serpent removed, the sun eclipsing, the dead rising from the earth in their multitudes.

He closed his fist around the gem. It was warm. Warmer than the jungle air. Warmer than it should have been.

Above the canopy, where the sun should have burned away the night and driven the dead back into their graves, there was nothing. The sky was empty. The stars had gone out. The darkness that pressed against Fizzleblast's shimmering barrier was not the darkness of night.

It was the darkness of forever.

Adjunct Professor Emeritus Aramil tucked the Eye of the Serpent into his breast pocket, next to the purple crystal he always wore, and said nothing to anyone.

Somewhere in the empty sky above Faerun, in the space where the sun had hung for longer than any mortal memory could reach, there was now only absence — and in the lightless temples and forgotten tombs across every continent, things that had slept for centuries opened their eyes and began to move.

Session Flow

Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip — Event Timeline

A chronological record of the expedition into the Chultian Jungle and the subsequent cosmic consequences of the retrieval of the Golden Snake.

Timeline Conventions

  • Session References: Events are tagged with [S1].
  • In-Game Time: The expedition began at roughly high noon and concluded shortly after sunset.
  • Event Significance:
  • ⚔️ Combat — Armed conflict
  • 🗣️ Social — Negotiation or deception
  • 🔍 Discovery — Puzzles, items, or lore
  • 📍 Travel — Relocation
  • ⚡ Turning Point — Major narrative shifts
  • 💀 Death/Loss — Defeats or casualties
  • 🏆 Victory/Achievement — Milestones

Active Campaign Timeline

Arc 1: The Forbidden Shores of Chult

Session 1 — The Temple of the Man and Crocodile

Event 1.1: The Chultian Briefing

  • Type: 📍 / 🗣️
  • In-Game Time: High Noon
  • Location: [[The Forbidden Shores of Chult]]
  • What Happened: The party arrived via canoe on the heavy, humid shores of Chult. [[Blue Orbit]] (acting as [[Fizzleblast]]) provided the mission objective: retrieve a golden, snake-shaped artifact from a tiered temple in exchange for whatever secondary loot the party could carry.
  • Participants: [[Moor]], [[Aramil]], [[Opal]], [[Lavender]], [[Bob]], [[Fizzleblast]], [[Niles]]
  • Cause: The party answered a public marketing flyer posted by the gnome explorer [[Fizzleblast]].
  • Consequences: Established the time-sensitive nature of the mission (canoes must return by noon the next day) and introduced the threat of the [[Red Fangs]]/[[Black Skulls]] rival group.

Event 1.2: The Attunement Accident

  • Type: 🔍
  • In-Game Time: ~12:15 PM
  • Location: Temple Exterior
  • What Happened: While unloading gear, the intern [[Niles]] tripped, shattering a Potion of Instant Attunement. The resulting magical splash caused various artifacts to instantly bind to the party members.
  • Participants: [[Moor]], [[Aramil]], [[Opal]], [[Lavender]], [[Bob]], [[Niles]]
  • Notable Details: [[Moor]] received Adamantine Plate; [[Bob]] received Slippers of Spider Climbing; [[Opal]] received a Pearl of Power; [[Lavender]] received Bracers of Defense; [[Aramil]] received Gloves of Thievery.

Event 1.3: The Girallon Gatekeepers

  • Type: ⚔️ / 🔍
  • In-Game Time: ~12:30 PM
  • Location: Temple Entrance
  • What Happened: Two four-armed Girallons attacked at the temple threshold. [[Moor]] and [[Opal]] utilized the temple's heliographic thermal mirrors to incinerate one beast. [[Aramil]] used Speak with Animals and Intimidation to terrify the second Girallon into fleeing.
  • Participants: [[Moor]], [[Aramil]], [[Opal]], [[Lavender]], [[Bob]]
  • Consequences: The party learned to manipulate the thermal ray mirrors, a mechanic vital for survival and puzzles within the temple.

Event 1.4: The Antechamber Trials

  • Type: 🔍
  • In-Game Time: ~1:00 PM
  • Location: [[Temple Antechamber]]
  • What Happened: The party discovered a room filled with gold piles and pressure-plate traps. [[Moor]] and [[Lavender]] used gold and heavy shields to weigh down plates, while [[Aramil]] and [[Bob]] managed the rope-and-counterweight system for the main door.
  • Participants: [[Moor]], [[Aramil]], [[Opal]], [[Lavender]], [[Bob]]
  • Discovery: [[Lavender]] found a Rod of the Pact Keeper; [[Moor]] found an Amulet of the Devout; [[Opal]] gained the Eldritch Claw Tattoo.

Event 1.5: The Blinding of Opal

  • Type: 💀 / 🔍
  • In-Game Time: ~2:30 PM
  • Location: The Prism Room
  • What Happened: While attempting to align a mirror to hit a central crystalline prism, [[Opal]] slipped and fell into a focused thermal beam. The radiant energy dealt significant damage and left her blinded.
  • Participants: [[Opal]], [[Moor]]
  • Consequences: [[Moor]] later utilized the Staff of Healing to restore [[Opal]]'s sight, depleting early resources.

Event 1.6: The Reliquary & The Rising Heat

  • Type: ⚡ / 🔍
  • In-Game Time: ~3:30 PM
  • Location: The Prism Room
  • What Happened: Guided by her patron, [[Lavender]] retrieved the [[Zephyr Reliquary]] from a high shelf.
  • Cause: A telepathic nudge from the Djinn [[Jezebel]].
  • Consequences: Removing the reliquary deactivated the temple's internal cooling system. The ambient temperature began to rise dangerously, threatening the party with fire damage every 10 minutes.

Event 1.7: The Poison Gas & The Bone Explosion

  • Type: ⚔️ / 🔍
  • In-Game Time: ~4:00 PM
  • Location: The Prism Room
  • What Happened: [[Moor]] breached a side door, accidentally triggering a skeleton horde and a noxious gas trap. [[Moor]] utilized Turn Undead to incinerate the skeletons, while [[Lavender]] used Gust of Wind to clear the poison.
  • Participants: [[Moor]], [[Lavender]], [[Aramil]], [[Bob]]
  • Consequences: The party suffered heavy damage from the gas but managed to align the mirrors to open the final portcullis.

Event 1.8: Retrieval of the Judgment Scale

  • Type: 🔍 / 🏆
  • In-Game Time: ~4:45 PM
  • Location: The Tier Hallway
  • What Happened: [[Bob]] performed a high-stakes swap (Indiana Jones style) using oil flasks to replace the [[Crocodile's Judgment Scale]] on a pressure-sensitive pedestal.
  • Participants: [[Bob]], [[Moor]]
  • Consequences: The party acquired a powerful artifact allowing a daily reroll.

Event 1.9: The T-Rex Heist

  • Type: 🔍 / 🏆
  • In-Game Time: Sunset
  • Location: The Temple Apex
  • What Happened: The party found a T-Rex sleeping under the [[Golden Snake Idol]]. [[Bob]] used his Cloak of the Bat and Boots of Striding and Springing to stealthily climb the archway and retrieve the idol without waking the beast.
  • Participants: [[Bob]], [[Aramil]], [[Opal]], [[Moor]], [[Lavender]]
  • Consequences: The primary objective was achieved, but a rival team of mages launched a fireball to wake the dinosaur and kill the party.

Event 1.10: The Flight from Chult

  • Type: ⚡ / 🏆
  • In-Game Time: Nightfall
  • Location: Temple Exterior / Fizzleblast's Bubble
  • What Happened: The party engaged in a desperate skill challenge to outrun the T-Rex and a stampede of undead. They reached [[Fizzleblast]]'s safety bubble just as the rival mages were devoured by the jungle's predators.
  • Participants: All PCs
  • Turning Point: During the escape, [[Aramil]] secretly pried the eye from the [[Golden Snake Idol]].
  • Consequences: The party survived with their loot, but [[Aramil]]'s theft of the eye plunged the continent of [[Faerun]] into eternal darkness.

Event Chains

Chain: The Cosmic Darkness

  1. [[Fizzleblast]] hires the party → 2. Party learns the idol is connected to the sun → 3. [[Aramil]] steals the idol's eye → CURRENT STATUS: [[Faerun]] is in eternal darkness; [[Aramil]] possesses the eye at [[Royal Community College]].

    2. Chain: The Environmental Hazard

    1. Party enters cooled temple → 2. [[Lavender]] takes the [[Zephyr Reliquary]] → 3. Temperature rises, causing damage → 4. Mirrored light fails at night → CURRENT STATUS: Resolved (Party escaped).

      2. Timeline by Character

      [[Aramil]]:

      • [S1] Used Speak with Animals to bypass a Girallon.
      • [S1] Deciphered the murals regarding the "Eternal Darkness" prophecy.
      • [S1] Downed by a Giant Snake; restored by [[Moor]].
      • [S1] Stole the Eye of the Serpent, fulfilling a dark personal ambition.

      [[Bob]]:

      • [S1] Used Slippers of Spider Climbing to navigate the mirrors.
      • [S1] Performed the swap for the [[Judgment Scale]].
      • [S1] Flew using the Cloak of the Bat to steal the [[Golden Snake Idol]].

      [[Opal]]:

      • [S1] Burned and blinded by the thermal array.
      • [S1] Used 23 Strength to shift massive stone mechanisms.
      • [S1] Critted a Giant Snake with the Bloodaxe.

      Timeline by Location

      • [S1] Central hub of the temple.
      • [S1] Site of the blinded PC accident.
      • [S1] Location where the internal AC (Reliquary) was removed.

Energy Peaks

Hype

Table Energy Peak Analysis — Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip (One-Shot)

Energy Detection Methodology

This analysis identifies high-energy moments through textual indicators in the transcript. The following signals are weighted:

Indicator Weight What It Looks Like
Rapid-fire exchanges High Multiple speakers in quick succession, short utterances (< 10 words each), minimal pauses
Exclamation marks Medium-High "YES!" "NO!" "Holy shit!" — emotional punctuation clusters
ALL CAPS High "WHAT" "NO WAY" "OH MY GOD" — the textual equivalent of shouting
Laughter markers Medium [laughter], "haha", "lmao", "I'm dying", or any indication of group laughter
Overlapping speech indicators High Multiple people responding to the same moment, cross-talk, interruptions
Emotional vocabulary Medium Swearing, superlatives ("that's the best thing ever"), emotional declarations
Short reactive utterances High "Oh no" "Wait" "What?!" "Dude" — verbal reactions, not full sentences
Exclamatory questions Medium "Did that just happen?!" "Are you serious?!" "HOW?!"
Table-wide participation Very High When ALL or nearly all players react to the same moment — the rarest and strongest signal
Contrast with surrounding text Medium A sudden shift from calm, measured dialogue to chaotic, rapid exchanges

Note: No single indicator is sufficient. True energy peaks combine multiple indicators simultaneously. A single "lol" is not a peak. Five people reacting in rapid succession with exclamations and laughter IS.

Top 10 Energy Peaks

Rank Peak Name What Happened Time/Location Trigger Duration (lines) Participants Peak Indicators Energy Score (1-10)
1 "The Escape by 4:20" The party completed the skill challenge escape sequence with exactly 4 minutes and 20 seconds to spare, barely surviving a gauntlet of traps, rival adventurers, and a rampaging T-Rex Final Quarter (~line 2995-3097) Arriving at the safety bubble just as the timer expired and the dead rose ~100 lines All 6 (Brandon, Kyle 1, Kyle 2, Shannon, Kelsey, Josh) Table-wide reaction, rapid-fire exchanges, exclamations, emotional vocabulary, contrast shift, short reactive utterances 10
2 "Opal Crits the Snake Blind" Blind Opal rolled a critical hit with her Great Axe on the giant constrictor snake, dealing 30 damage and killing it while it was squeezing Aramil unconscious Third Quarter (~line 2078-2097) Shannon rolling a critical hit while her character was blind ~20 lines All 6 Exclamation clusters, rapid-fire exchanges, table-wide reaction, emotional vocabulary, laughter 9
3 "Squirtons and Gushers" The entire currency joke escalated when Moor introduced "squirtons" and Bob asked "How many squirtons in a gusher?" sending the table into chaos First Quarter (~line 8-18) Bob's "how many squirtons in a gusher?" line ~12 lines All 6 Laughter markers ("hahaha"), rapid-fire exchanges, exclamations ("Oh my god"), table-wide participation 8.5
4 "Bob Goes Batman" Bob grabbed the golden idol from above a sleeping T-Rex, jumped off holding his Cloak of the Bat, and flew away — only to get hit by a fireball from the rival party Fourth Quarter (~line 2970-2995) Bob leaping off the archway holding his cloak and declaring he's flying ~25 lines All 6 Exclamation clusters, emotional vocabulary ("Oh my god, I'm sideways! I'm a motherfucking knight"), rapid-fire exchanges, high stakes contrast 8.5
5 "The Sun Goes Out" Aramil secretly stole the golden eye from the snake idol, and the DM revealed this plunged all of Faerun into eternal darkness Final wrap-up (~line 3100-3112) Brandon's reveal: "You have plunged the entire continent into eternal darkness" ~13 lines All 6 Emotional vocabulary, exclamations, short reactive utterances ("Not cool, man," "You monster"), contrast shift from triumph to horror 8
6 "Aramil Gets Squeezed to -10" The giant constrictor snake grappled Aramil and dealt 22 damage, dropping him to negative 10 HP — the first character to go down Third Quarter (~line 2058-2075) The DM announcing 22 constriction damage ~18 lines All 6 Exclamation clusters ("Oh my God, that's a lot"), rapid-fire exchanges, emotional vocabulary, multiple simultaneous reactions 7.5
7 "Everybody Gets Poisoned (14 Damage)" The toxic gas trap hit the entire party for 14 damage each, nearly wiping them, with multiple players announcing their desperately low HP totals Second Quarter (~line 3598-3632) DM rolling 3d8+4 and announcing 14 damage to everyone ~35 lines All 6 Exclamation clusters ("Oh my god"), emotional vocabulary ("Fuck"), rapid-fire HP announcements, table-wide participation 7.5
8 "If the Men Find Out We Can Shapeshift" Lavender's iconic quote about Tika's shapeshifting abilities had the table erupting — later voted Quote of the Session Third Quarter (~line 1755-1762) Kelsey delivering the line "If the men find out we can shapeshift, they're going to tell the church" ~8 lines Shannon, Kelsey, Kyle 2, Brandon Laughter, rapid-fire reactions, emotional vocabulary, sharp contrast with combat discussion 7
9 "Mirror Melts the Gorilla" Moor turned the thermal mirror onto the Gorallon, and it melted through the creature in spectacular fashion — "it smells delicious... like cooked steak" First Quarter (~line 333-344) Brandon describing the gorilla being melted by the focused beam ~12 lines Brandon, Kyle 1, Kyle 2, Shannon, Kelsey Exclamation clusters, laughter, emotional vocabulary, overlapping reactions 6.5
10 "Nat 1 Fireball Sends Rivals Fleeing" During the final escape, one of the rival mages rolled a Nat 1 on the save against Aramil's Phantasmal Force, causing him to panic, grab his buddy, and flee Final Quarter (~line 3071-3078) Brandon announcing the Nat 1 result ~8 lines All 6 Rapid-fire exchanges, exclamations, short reactive utterances, emotional release (tension breaking) 6

Deep Dive: The #1 Energy Peak

"The Escape by 4:20"

Location: The entire temple, from the inner chamber back through the trapped hallway to Fizzleblast's safety bubble on the beach

Trigger: The DM starting a real-time skill challenge with exactly 4 minutes and 20 seconds on the clock, combined with the T-Rex waking up, the rival party attacking, and the dead literally rising from the ground

Timestamp: Approximately 3.5-4 hours into the session (final 15-20 minutes of play)

The Build-Up

The entire session had been building toward this moment. The party had spent roughly three hours methodically exploring the temple — solving mirror puzzles, fighting a constrictor snake, managing poison gas, looting treasure, and debating plans. The pace was deliberately measured, with long stretches of collaborative problem-solving and frequent tangential conversations. The group had discovered the golden snake idol above a sleeping T-Rex, spent agonizing minutes debating how to retrieve it, and then Bob executed a daring heist by climbing up, grabbing it, and Batman-gliding down with his Cloak of the Bat. But then a fireball hit from the rival party, the T-Rex woke up, and Brandon announced the skill challenge with a timer that was already running. The contrast between the leisurely exploration pace and the sudden urgency was immense — the session's pacing went from a slow boil to a full explosion in seconds.

The Moment

Brandon declared: "You have exactly four minutes and 20 seconds to reach Fizzle Blast's bubble." Then: "Every 10 seconds, one of you has to make a skill check... On three fails, one of you dies." This transformed the game from a collaborative puzzle into a frantic relay race. Each player had to volunteer for skill checks in rapid succession — athletics to sprint through the closing door, acrobatics to leap over pressure plates, strength checks to pull up the massive stone door — and nobody could go twice until everyone had gone. The tension was compounded by the ongoing threat: blades shot up from pressure plates dealing 13 damage to Kyle 2 and Kyle 1 (two failures already — one more and someone dies), a rival mage dimension-doored in front of them, and the dead were literally clawing out of the ground behind them. The real-time timer added a meta-layer of urgency that collapsed the boundary between players and characters.

The Eruption

The cascade began when Bob's 36-foot superhuman jump cleared the trapped hallway, but the stone door was shut. The table erupted into simultaneous action and dialogue — Kelsey shouting about Eldritch Blasting the door, Shannon ready with her Bloodaxe, Kyle 1 and Opal pulling ropes with combined strength checks. When the rival mages dimension-doored in front of them, multiple players spoke at once: "Oh my god, I have a Blood Axe!" / "Phantasmal Force, Blood Axe, command" / "Flee!" Kyle 2's Phantasmal Force made one mage see a pack of raptors, and Brandon's Nat 1 announcement sent the rival fleeing. Shannon flipped them off as the party sprinted into the bubble. The energy peaked with overlapping exclamations of relief: "Oh my god!" / "I'm so fucking stressed right now" / "Yay!" / "Those are some freaking rolls at the end" / "I'm just so impressed that we actually did it." The post-escape conversation sustained high energy for another 100+ lines as the group processed what had just happened, with everyone talking over each other about how close it was.

Why This Was Peak Energy

This moment was peak energy because it combined every possible tension multiplier simultaneously. First, the real-time timer created genuine urgency that the players could not strategize away — a radical departure from the session's previous leisurely pace. Second, the stakes were lethal: three failures meant a character death, and they had already burned two. Third, every single player was actively participating in rapid succession, each making critical rolls that affected everyone's survival. Fourth, the narrative convergence was extraordinary — the T-Rex, the rival party, the rising dead, the trapped hallway, and the ticking clock all converged into a single sprint. And fifth, it was cathartic: after three-plus hours of careful deliberation and debate (which multiple players joked about being "not our style"), the final escape proved they could act decisively when it mattered. The relief and disbelief in the post-escape conversation ("I can't believe we made it with four and a half minutes") shows a group that genuinely thought they might fail — and the release of that tension is what made this the highest-energy moment of the session.

The Transcript Excerpt

```

DM: You have exactly four minutes and 20 seconds to reach Fizzle Blast's bubble.

DM: Okay, run.

Bob: Run.

DM: Every 10 seconds, one of you has to make a skill check. We're going to do a skill challenge. It's a skill challenge. You're running through. Cool. I'm

Opal: going to drop my shield, I think. On

DM: three fails, one of you dies.

Aramil: Okay. Do they have to be skills specifically, or can spells count as skills?

[...]

DM: All right, the swords are still blocking your way from the portcullis. Oh no, fuck that sword. Kyle has to do this. One person that can help him.

Opal: Yeah, okay, I can help. All right, go ahead.

Opal: 19. No, I'm sorry. 17. 17.

Moor: 19.

DM: 19 is exactly what you needed.

[...]

DM: Three and ten. You step on the plates and you each are going to take... 3d8 damage from the blades.

DM: That is 13 damage each. That is two failures. One more. And somebody dies.

[...]

Aramil: I'm going to cast Phantasmal Force on it.

Moor: I'm going to cast Command to stop one of our friends. If I see them.

[...]

DM: Oh, Nat 1 runs and grabs his buddy and pulls him with him because it's a Nat 1.

Opal: You run. I flip him off as we run.

DM: And you make it barely into... Safety!

DM: Oh my god!

Lavender: Actual timer on that.

DM: You jump into the bubble as the entire Chultian jungle starts to raise the dead come out of the ground...

[...]

Opal: I cannot believe we won. I can't

DM: believe we did it.

Aramil: It was down to it.

Bob: There's only ever been one person.

Lavender: I'm just so impressed that we actually did it.

Opal: Wow, that was down to the wire.

```

Additional Peak Writeups

#2: "Opal Crits the Snake Blind"

Shannon's Opal had been blinded by a thermal mirror beam just minutes earlier — 14 radiant damage directly to the eyes. When the giant constrictor snake grappled Aramil and squeezed him to -10 HP, the situation was desperate. Shannon then rolled a critical hit with her Great Axe while blind, and the table exploded into a math frenzy: "Plus 12 is 24. Plus 6 is 30. No, right?" / "Yes. 30. You're going to off it" / "Yay!" The combination of a blinded character delivering a killing blow, the clutch rescue of a downed party member, and the cathartic release after a tense combat made this the second-highest energy moment. Shannon's description of chopping through the coiled snake "like a cake, but less delicately" sealed it.

#3: "Squirtons and Gushers"

The very first energy spike came within the opening minutes, when the DM mentioned the party might be "hurting for a squirting" and Kelsey immediately said "that's what went in my head before you said it, 'squirtin' hahaha." Shannon and Josh joined with laughter ("Oh my god, hahaha"), and Kyle 1 doubled down in character: "It's not weird, that's just the name of the currency in the area. They're called squirtons." When Kyle 2 asked about the "tier of the currency" and Josh asked "How many squirtons in a gusher?", the DM broke: "Thank you, Bob, for bringing it home." This was pure comedy energy — improvised, collaborative, and escalating. It set the comedic tone for the entire session.

#4-10: Brief Descriptions

#4: "Bob Goes Batman" — Bob grabbed the idol from above the sleeping T-Rex, leaped off declaring "I'm a motherfucking knight," and promptly got hit by a fireball from the rival party. The whiplash from triumph to danger in two seconds generated massive table-wide energy.

#5: "The Sun Goes Out" — In the post-game wrap-up, Kyle 2 casually revealed he had stolen the golden eye. Brandon's deadpan reveal that this plunged Faerun into eternal darkness shifted the room from celebration to shocked laughter. "Not cool, man" / "You monster" / "Seems like we need to play that next."

#6: "Aramil Gets Squeezed to -10" — The first character downing of the session. Kyle 2's announcement of "I am at negative 10" followed by rapid overlapping concern and strategy showed the table's genuine investment in each other's survival.

#7: "Everybody Gets Poisoned" — The toxic gas hitting the entire party for 14 damage created a cascade of panicked HP announcements: "I have 11 hit points" / "I have 12" / "We're doing so good" (dripping with sarcasm). The table-wide damage created genuine shared dread.

#8: "If the Men Find Out We Can Shapeshift" — Kelsey's perfectly timed reference landed so well it was voted Quote of the Session. A comedic peak that emerged organically from discussion about Tika's shapeshifting abilities during snake combat.

#9: "Mirror Melts the Gorilla" — The first creative environmental kill. Moor turning the thermal mirror onto the Gorallon and Brandon describing the creature melting ("it smells delicious... like cooked steak. Plus burnt hair") generated excited reactions and established the mirror mechanic that would define the rest of the session.

#10: "Nat 1 Sends Rivals Fleeing" — During the escape, the rival mage's Nat 1 on the save against Phantasmal Force — causing him to panic and drag his ally away — was a perfect tension-release moment that drew cheers and laughter.

Energy Pattern Analysis

Peak Distribution

Session Segment Peaks in This Segment Avg Peak Energy Dominant Peak Type
First Quarter (lines 1-800) 2 7.5 Comedy
Second Quarter (lines 800-1600) 1 7.5 Chaos
Third Quarter (lines 1600-2400) 3 7.8 Combat / Comedy
Final Quarter (lines 2400-3206) 4 8.1 Combat / Dramatic

Peak Types

Type Count Examples
Combat Peak 3 Opal critting the snake blind (#2), Aramil squeezed to -10 (#6), mirror melts gorilla (#9)
Comedy Peak 2 Squirtons and Gushers (#3), Shapeshift quote (#8)
Dramatic Peak 1 The Sun Goes Out (#5)
Discovery Peak 0 —
Dice Peak 1 Nat 1 sends rivals fleeing (#10)
Social Peak 0 —
Chaos Peak 3 The Escape by 4:20 (#1), Bob Goes Batman (#4), Everybody Gets Poisoned (#7)

Energy Flow Narrative

The session's energy pattern resembled a classic adventure movie structure: a playful, comedy-heavy opening that gradually gave way to escalating danger and a breathless finale. The first quarter was dominated by comedic energy — the squirtons bit, character introductions with Fizzleblast's eccentric personality, and lighthearted banter about inventory management. Baseline energy during this stretch was moderate and warm, with frequent laughs but no genuine tension. The second quarter saw the party settling into puzzle-solving mode, with long stretches of deliberate, measured conversation as they worked through pressure plates and mirror mechanics. Energy dipped to its lowest sustained point during the extended mechanical discussions about who should push which plate and how to weigh them down with gold — functional but not electric.

The third quarter marked a decisive shift as combat entered the picture. The constrictor snake fight brought the session's first genuine danger — Aramil getting squeezed unconscious, Opal fighting blind, the toxic gas threatening a party wipe. Peaks began clustering more tightly, with combat tension generating sharper spikes than the puzzle-solving had. The comedy peaks during this stretch (the shapeshift quote) hit harder because they emerged from high-tension contexts, providing relief and contrast.

The final quarter was a sustained escalation with almost no cool-down between peaks. From the T-Rex discovery to Bob's heist to the fireball to the skill challenge to the reveal of eternal darkness, the energy ratcheted upward continuously. The skill challenge itself was the session's definitive climax — a concentrated burst of maximum-stakes, rapid-fire participation that involved every player. The session ended on a high note, with the post-game wrap-up sustaining excited energy as the group processed their narrow survival and Kyle 2's secret betrayal provided one last twist.

Group Energy Dynamics

Player Peaks Participated In (of 10) Peak Initiations Energy Role
Brandon (DM/Blue Orbit) 10/10 4 Catalyst
Kyle 1 (Moor) 8/10 1 Amplifier
Kyle 2 (Aramil) 9/10 2 Catalyst
Shannon (Opal) 10/10 2 Amplifier
Kelsey (Lavender) 9/10 2 Responder
Josh (Bob) 9/10 2 Catalyst

Energy Roles Explained:

  • Brandon (DM) — Catalyst. As expected from a DM, Brandon initiated the most peaks through reveals, descriptions, and mechanical triggers (the poison gas, the T-Rex, the skill challenge timer, the Nat 1 result). But Brandon also functioned as an exceptional energy amplifier, matching the table's comedic energy beat-for-beat (the squirtons bit, Fizzleblast's personality). His DM style was reactive and collaborative, feeding off player energy rather than suppressing it.
  • Kyle 1 (Moor) — Amplifier. Kyle 1 was present in most peaks and consistently elevated energy through dry, deadpan humor ("Why use many word when small word do trick") and strategic competence (turning the mirror on the gorilla, casting Turn Undead on the skeletons). His energy role was primarily supportive — he rarely initiated explosions but his reactions and character choices consistently raised the ceiling of moments other people started.
  • Kyle 2 (Aramil) — Catalyst. Kyle 2 was responsible for two of the session's most distinctive peaks — the speaking-with-animals persuasion attempt on the gorilla and the stolen golden eye that plunged the world into darkness. His character choices consistently created energy through chaos, unpredictability, and a willingness to commit to risky or morally questionable decisions. His "I'm cool with that" response to learning the idol might cause eternal darkness was a masterclass in character commitment.
  • Shannon (Opal) — Amplifier. Shannon was present in every single peak and her reactions were among the most expressive in the transcript ("Oh my god," "Oh my gosh," "I cannot believe we won"). She amplified energy primarily through enthusiastic vocal reactions and in-character commitment — going blind, raging at mirrors, critting the snake. Her high-energy personality kept baseline energy elevated even during slower stretches.
  • Kelsey (Lavender) — Responder. Kelsey was consistently present in peaks and contributed key moments (the shapeshift quote, the Hellish Rebuke double-kill, the Gust of Wind that saved the party from poison). Her energy profile was steady rather than explosive — she maintained a consistent level of engagement that anchored the group during both high and low moments. Her strategic thinking ("Should we pop into my genie lamp?") often redirected chaotic energy into productive channels.
  • Josh (Bob) — Catalyst. Josh initiated multiple peaks through bold character choices — the "squirtons in a gusher" line, the distraction play (screaming and running to draw baboons), the Batman idol heist. Bob's energy came in concentrated bursts: quiet for stretches, then delivering a moment that changed the trajectory of the scene. His willingness to take dramatic solo risks (climbing up to grab the idol, flying through a fireball) created the conditions for the table's biggest reactions.

Session Energy Summary

The Session's Energy Identity

If this session had a personality, it would be a buddy heist film — think Ocean's Eleven meets Indiana Jones, directed by someone who keeps forgetting to yell "cut" because the cast is too entertaining off-script. The session's defining quality was a persistent undercurrent of collaborative comedy that elevated every encounter above its mechanical stakes. Pressure plate puzzles became absurdist debates about gold economics. A lethal constrictor snake fight became a conversation about snake diplomacy. A ticking clock escape became a highlight reel of impossible rolls. The group's natural inclination toward tangential conversation and debate — which they openly acknowledged as "not our style" to be efficient — actually built the session's identity: the contrast between their leisurely deliberation and the DM's relentless timer created the tension that made the finale electrifying.

What Generated the Most Energy

This group responds most strongly to chaos under pressure — specifically, moments where things go wrong (or nearly wrong) in ways that demand immediate, table-wide response. The top four peaks all share this DNA: the escape skill challenge, a blind crit, a Batman heist interrupted by a fireball, and the apocalyptic theft of a cursed artifact. Pure combat (without narrative stakes or comedy) generated less energy than combat combined with absurdity (fighting blind, talking to a snake, melting gorillas). Comedy peaks also ranked highly, but the group's comedy is improvisational and collaborative rather than performative — it emerges from situation and character, not from set-up/punchline structures. The group's comedy and chaos peaks often overlapped, suggesting that for this table, laughter and danger are not separate categories but mutually reinforcing states.

Recommendations for the DM

  1. The real-time timer was this session's most powerful tool. The skill challenge escape generated the highest energy of the entire session by an enormous margin. This group's natural tendency to debate and deliberate means that external time pressure transforms their play experience — it forces the decisiveness that creates dramatic moments. Incorporating timed elements (not necessarily real-time, but clearly urgent) into future sessions will reliably generate peak energy. The DM should continue to build in "you have X minutes" mechanics for climactic sequences.
    1. Environmental puzzles that allow creative combat solutions generate more energy than straight fights. The thermal mirror kills (melting the gorilla, redirecting beams through snake-eye statues) were more exciting to this table than standard attack-roll-damage exchanges. The group lit up whenever they could interact with the environment rather than just rolling attacks. Future sessions should continue to feature terrain and environmental hazards that players can weaponize — this group will find creative uses for them.
      1. This group thrives on information asymmetry and secret choices. Kyle 2 stealing the golden eye was a session-defining moment precisely because it was a unilateral secret decision that affected everyone. The rival party lurking above (heard through Tika's invisible scouting) also generated strong energy. Giving individual players secret objectives, hidden knowledge, or the opportunity for covert action will create the inter-player dramatic tension that this table clearly enjoys. The DM should look for opportunities to hand individual players notes or private information that create later reveal moments.
Campaign Codex

Codex

NPC Profiles Wiki — Charisma Company: Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip

Compiled from the Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip one-shot session transcript.

DM: Blue Orbit (Brandon)

Fizzleblast

```yaml

type: npc

name: "Fizzleblast"

aliases:

  • "Mr. Fizzleblast"
  • "Fizzle"

race: "Gnome"

occupation: "Wizard / Expedition Patron / Leader of [[Charisma Company]]"

affiliation: "[[Charisma Company]]"

location: "[[The Shore]] / [[Fizzle Field]] Bubble"

status: "Alive"

dispositiontoparty: "Friendly (Transactional)"

first_appearance: "Session 1 (One-shot)"

last_appearance: "Session 1 (One-shot)"

sessions_appeared: [1]

importance: "Major"

tags:

  • npc
  • charisma-company
  • quest-giver
  • patron
  • gnome
  • wizard

```

Fizzleblast

"I hate repeating myself. Okay, all right, it dries out my throat talking too much anyways."

Overview

Fizzleblast is a neurotic, self-important gnome wizard who serves as the patron and quest giver for the entire expedition. He hired the party through a combination of private recruitment and an accidental public posting on "Mercenaries, Murders, and Mothers-in-Law's" public forums to retrieve a golden snake-shaped artifact from a [[Temple of the Man and Crocodile|Chultan jungle temple]]. He promised the party they could keep any loot they found, as long as they delivered the artifact to him.

Despite positioning himself as the expedition leader, Fizzleblast is fundamentally a coward. He stayed safely ensconced in his patented "[[Fizzle Field]]" protective bubble on the beach while the party risked their lives inside the temple. He spent the entire session sleeping with a teddy bear while his hired adventurers nearly died multiple times. His relationship with the party is purely transactional — he needs the artifact, they need the pay and loot.

Fizzleblast is the driving force behind the entire adventure. Without his research discovering the temple, his funding for the expedition, and his magical equipment loans, the party would never have been there. He is simultaneously essential and useless — the classic "employer who stays in the office while the field team does the dangerous work."

Appearance & Mannerisms

  • Physical Description: A gnome with a big beard and huge, oversized spectacles. Wears a cashmere robe and hat that are both far too large for him — the hat would cover his entire head if not for the massive spectacles holding it up. Wears nice leather boots that are clearly ornamental rather than practical.
  • Notable Mannerisms: Obsessive about his cashmere garments and their preservation. Berates his intern Niles constantly. Speaks in rapid, rambling sentences that loop back on themselves. Gets flustered easily and contradicts himself mid-instruction.
  • First Impression: Standing at the bow of the canoe, yelling at [[Niles]] about the cashmere getting wet. Immediately establishes himself as vain, demanding, and more concerned with comfort than the dangerous mission ahead.

[!note] Description Completeness

The DM provided a solid physical description of Fizzleblast including his oversized clothing, spectacles, beard, and boots. The description paints a clear picture of a small creature drowning in expensive, impractical garments.

Role in Story

Primary quest giver and expedition patron. Fizzleblast is the narrative engine of the one-shot — he discovered the temple, hired the party, provided equipment, established the time constraint (canoe rental deadline), and set up the safety perimeter. He also inadvertently created the rival party threat by accidentally posting the expedition details on a public forum instead of private channels.

Key Contributions

  • Hired the party to retrieve the [[Golden Snake Idol]] from the [[Temple of the Man and Crocodile]] (pre-session)
  • Provided magical items via the "loot bag" that [[Niles]] dropped, scattering equipment for the party (Session 1)
  • Established the [[Fizzle Field]] safety perimeter on the shore — the party's only safe haven (Session 1)
  • Set the time constraint: canoes must be returned by noon the next day, party must be back before dark (Session 1)
  • Warned the party about the rival group and the heliographic thermal arrays / mirror defense system (Session 1)
  • Accidentally created the rival party threat by posting on public forums (Session 1)
  • Was asleep with a teddy bear when the party returned with the idol (Session 1)
  • Refused to let party members into the [[Fizzle Field]] without the idol: "Not without the idol." (Session 1)

Information Held

What He Told the Party

Information Context Session Reliability
The artifact is golden and snake-shaped, located in the back room/vaults of the temple Briefing on the shore 1 Appears truthful — artifact matched description
The party can keep all other loot they find Part of the hiring agreement 1 Appears truthful — no contradiction observed
Heliographic thermal arrays (mirrors) focus sunlight into death rays that burn anything Warning before entry 1 Confirmed true — party witnessed the mirrors melting gorallons
A rival group ("Red Fangs, Black Skulls, something edgy") is about an hour behind them Warning on arrival 1 Confirmed true — rival party appeared later
He accidentally posted the expedition on public forums instead of private channels Admitted during briefing 1 Appears truthful — explains rival party's presence
Canoes are rented for 24 hours; must be returned by noon Logistical constraint 1 Unverified but accepted as true
The [[Fizzle Field]] repels organic matter, undead matter, and "aggressive sales tactics" Description of the safety bubble 1 Confirmed true — undead could not penetrate it
He does not know exactly what the artifact is, only that it exists and is snake-themed Briefing 1 Appears truthful — his description was vague
"Do not press the--" (cut off by entering the bubble) Interrupted warning 1 Unverified — he was cut off mid-sentence; likely referring to a trap mechanism in the temple

What He Might Know (Analytical Inference)

Potential Information Basis for Inference Confidence
Likely knows more about the temple's history and the [[Golden Snake Idol]]'s significance than he shared He researched the temple enough to organize an expedition; his briefing was suspiciously vague about the artifact's nature MEDIUM
May know about the curse/darkness consequence of removing the idol His interrupted warning ("Do not press the--") and his general research suggest awareness of dangers he did not fully disclose MEDIUM
Likely knows the identity of the rival group He saw them at the canoe rental place and seemed to recognize them as a competing outfit LOW
May have intentionally posted on public forums to create a distraction or competing group The "accidental" posting is convenient; having rivals could serve as expendable scouts or cannon fodder LOW

[!warning] Inference Disclaimer

"What They Might Know" entries are analytical inferences based on context, not confirmed facts. The DM may have different intentions for this NPC.

Dialogue Summary

Session 1

  • Topic: Mission Briefing
  • Explained the party was hired to retrieve a golden, snake-shaped artifact from the temple vaults
  • Emphasized they could keep all other loot: "Touch nothing but the lamp. I mean, wrong franchise."
  • Confused himself repeatedly during the briefing, mixing up Aladdin references
  • Key quote: "I hate repeating myself. Okay, all right, it dries out my throat talking too much anyways."
  • Topic: Logistics and Constraints
  • Canoes rented for 24 hours, must leave at sunrise: "Not 12:01. Noon."
  • Established time pressure and rental fee concerns
  • Key quote: "I don't want to lose my deposit, okay?"
  • Topic: Rival Party Warning
  • Admitted to accidentally posting expedition details publicly
  • Warned of a rival group approximately one hour behind them
  • Dismissed his own danger with dark humor: "They'll definitely kill you guys. I'm too important to die, so they'll probably ransom me."
  • Topic: Safety Equipment
  • Introduced the [[Fizzle Field]] as "patent pending"
  • Described it as repelling "organic matter, undead matter, and aggressive sales tactics"
  • Positioned himself in a "command position" to "supervise the perimeter" and "guard the canoes"
  • Topic: Magical Equipment Distribution
  • Ordered [[Niles]] to bring the bag of magical items
  • Items scattered when Niles tripped, leading to the Potion of Instant Attunement event
  • Initially wanted items returned but relented: "Consider it a signing bonus."
  • Topic: Mirror Warning (Interrupted)
  • Began to warn the party about something: "Oh, and whatever you do, do not press the--"
  • Was cut off when he stepped into the [[Fizzle Field]] bubble, which muted all sound

Connections

Relationships to Other Characters

Character Nature of Relationship Details Source
[[Niles]] Employer (abusive) Berates Niles constantly; threatens to "polymorph you into a tombstone" if he drops things; treats him as a pack mule and personal footbridge DM narration
[[The Rival Party]] Competitor Aware of their existence; accidentally alerted them to the expedition DM narration / Fizzleblast dialogue
[[Charisma Company]] Founder/Leader Runs the organization that hired the party DM narration

Disposition Toward Each PC

PC Disposition Basis Trajectory
[[Moor]] Neutral — business Called him "Bravo Rock" and confused his race; standard employer-employee dynamic Stable
[[Opal]] Amused — patronizing Called her "Oaf" instead of Opal; described her as "Paleolithic chic" Stable
[[Aramil]] Dismissive — mocking Called his name "Arrow Mill" and compared it to "budget laundry detergent" Stable
[[Lavender]] Warm — practical Hung his sun hat on her horns; she was agreeable and accommodating; complimented the cashmere Stable
[[Bob]] Neutral — indifferent Minimal direct interaction beyond the briefing Stable

Faction Connections

Faction Role Status
[[Charisma Company]] Founder and leader Active

DM Voice Notes

[!tip] Performance Guide

This section exists to help the DM portray this NPC consistently across sessions.

Voice & Speech

  • Accent / Voice Quality: Rapid, high-pitched, flustered. The DM portrayed Fizzleblast with a hurried, nasal quality — the voice of someone who is always slightly overwhelmed and overly impressed with himself.
  • Speech Patterns: Rambling, self-interrupting sentences. Starts instructions, contradicts himself, restarts, gets confused, and then blames the listener. Uses "all right" and "okay" as constant verbal bridges. Frequently references himself in third person through his demands.
  • Verbal Tics: Trails off mid-thought and picks up new threads. Says "right?" seeking validation. Repeats the last word of his sentences for emphasis.
  • Vocabulary Level: Educated but scattered. Uses formal words ("heliographic thermal arrays") interspersed with casual panic ("I'm too important to die"). Mixes franchise references accidentally ("Touch nothing but the lamp — wrong franchise").

Emotional Baseline & Range

  • Default Demeanor: Neurotic, self-important, vaguely panicked. Always acting as though the situation is both beneath him and slightly beyond his control.
  • When Pleased: Brief moments of generosity ("Consider it a signing bonus") quickly followed by self-serving justification.
  • When Angered: Directed entirely at [[Niles]]. Becomes shrill and threatening ("I swear I will polymorph you into a tombstone").
  • When Frightened: Retreats to safety and rationalizes it as leadership ("It's really a command position"). Was literally asleep in the bubble with a teddy bear while the party fought for their lives.

Behavioral Patterns

  • Body Language: Theatrical gestures from someone who thinks they are more imposing than they are. Waves arms while giving instructions. Points dramatically at things.
  • Habits: Obsesses over the condition of his cashmere. Delegates all physical labor and danger to others. Falls asleep at critical moments.
  • Tells: When nervous, deflects with rapid-fire instructions. When caught being unhelpful, frames his inaction as strategic leadership.

Portrayal Summary

Fizzleblast is a tiny dictator in cashmere. Speak fast, pitched up, with the energy of a frazzled middle manager who desperately wants to seem important. He starts sentences confidently and then spirals into confusion. He genuinely believes he is the most important person on the expedition, and his self-preservation instincts are completely unashamed. When voicing him, interrupt yourself constantly — start an instruction, get sidetracked by a complaint about comfort, remember you were saying something important, contradict yourself, then blame the listener for being confused. He is equal parts Aladdin's merchant, a helicopter boss, and a cowardly lion in a cashmere robe.

Unresolved Threads

  • [ ] What was his interrupted warning? "Do not press the--" was cut off by the bubble. What was he about to say?
  • [ ] Does he know the [[Golden Snake Idol]] is connected to the sun and its removal causes eternal darkness?
  • [ ] Was the "accidental" public posting truly an accident, or did he want a competing team for some reason?
  • [ ] What is [[Charisma Company]]'s broader operation beyond this expedition?
  • [ ] What does he intend to do with the idol once he receives it?
  • [ ] Does he know that [[Aramil]] stole the [[Eye of the Serpent]]?
  • [ ] How will he react to the sun no longer rising?

Last updated after Session 1 (One-shot). Profile compiled from the Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip session transcript.

Niles

"I'm coming, I'm coming, Mr. Fizzleblast, I'm on my way, I'm sorry, I'm sorry."

Overview

Niles is [[Fizzleblast]]'s long-suffering intern, a prepubescent red-haired youth who serves as pack mule, personal footbridge, and general target of abuse. He is responsible for the "Loot Explosion" — the inciting event that scattered magical items across the beach and triggered the Potion of Instant Attunement, binding random equipment to each party member. By the end of the session, he was found on the floor of the [[Fizzle Field]] bubble, shaking from something he witnessed during the night. Despite his minor presence, Niles is the catalyst for the party's initial equipment distribution and a source of genuine pathos amidst the comedy.

Appearance & Mannerisms

  • Physical Description: Prepubescent, red hair, small frame struggling with loads far too heavy for him. Going through puberty.
  • Notable Mannerisms: Apologizes constantly. Struggles physically with everything. Trips over environmental hazards (stepped on a crab, causing the loot explosion).
  • First Impression: Seen being berated by Fizzleblast while hauling heavy sacks from the canoes. Fizzleblast made him lie down as a human bridge so others could disembark without getting wet.

Role in Story

Catalyst and comic relief. Niles's clumsiness directly caused the Potion of Instant Attunement to shatter, binding random magical items to each party member. This was the foundational equipment event of the entire session. His suffering at Fizzleblast's hands provides both humor and a subtle commentary on the wizard's character.

Key Contributions

  • Carried the Bag of Holding containing magical items and the Potion of Instant Attunement (Session 1)
  • Tripped on a crab and flung the loot sack, causing the "Loot Explosion" that distributed equipment (Session 1)
  • Served as Fizzleblast's human bridge for disembarking the canoe (Session 1)
  • Was found shaking on the floor at the end of the session, having witnessed something traumatic during the night (Session 1)

Information Held

What He Told the Party

Information Context Session Reliability
No direct information provided to the party Niles spoke only to Fizzleblast and only in apologetic fragments 1 N/A

What He Might Know (Analytical Inference)

Potential Information Basis for Inference Confidence
May know details about [[Fizzleblast]]'s research and the true nature of the idol Works closely as Fizzleblast's assistant; would have helped prepare the expedition MEDIUM
Witnessed something terrifying during the night that left him shaking Found on the floor of the bubble in a traumatized state HIGH — confirmed he saw something, unknown what

[!warning] Inference Disclaimer

"What They Might Know" entries are analytical inferences based on context, not confirmed facts. The DM may have different intentions for this NPC.

All PCs Neutral — minimal interaction Niles did not interact directly with any PC; he was entirely focused on Fizzleblast's demands Stable

Faction Connections

Faction Role Status
[[Charisma Company]] Intern / Graduate Assistant Active (presumably)

DM Voice Notes

[!tip] Performance Guide

Voice & Speech

  • Accent / Voice Quality: High-pitched, breathless, perpetually apologetic. The voice of someone who is always running, always late, and always in trouble.
  • Speech Patterns: Short, stuttering sentences. Repeats apologies. Speaks in bursts between physical exertion.
  • Verbal Tics: "I'm sorry, I'm sorry" as a constant refrain.

Portrayal Summary

Niles is pure slapstick pathos. Voice him breathless and panicked — he is always one mistake away from being polymorphed. His lines are short and apologetic, delivered while stumbling, carrying, or falling. He never speaks to the party directly. His entire existence revolves around Fizzleblast's demands. At the end of the session, he is found shaking and silent — a stark contrast to his earlier frantic energy. Play the shift: early Niles is comedic; end-of-session Niles is haunting.

Unresolved Threads

  • [ ] What did Niles witness during the night that left him shaking on the floor?
  • [ ] Is he aware of the eternal darkness that has fallen?
  • [ ] What is his actual relationship to Fizzleblast — is he a willing intern or effectively indentured?

Tika

"Hey! Meow."

Overview

Tika is [[Lavender]]'s imp familiar, a small fiend bound through her Pact of the Chain warlock abilities. Despite being a demon, Tika has a comedic personality — the players gave him a New Jersey accent and sassy demeanor. Tika served as a critical tactical asset throughout the session, scouting high areas, retrieving the [[Zephyr Reliquary]], performing reconnaissance on the rival party, and fighting the [[Giant Constrictor Snake]]. He was killed during the snake fight (paralyzed and bitten by the constrictor) but was resummoned via a 10-minute ritual cast by Lavender.

Appearance & Mannerisms

  • Physical Description: A "little, tiny devil demon" — small imp form. Can shapeshift into a raven, rat, or spider. Appeared primarily in imp form and raven form during the session.
  • Notable Mannerisms: Sassy, willing to follow orders with a hint of personality. The players characterized him with a New Jersey accent.
  • First Impression: Appeared during the gorallon fight, described by Lavender as "a little imp. His name's Tika. Like a little, little, tiny devil demon."

Role in Story

Scout, combatant, and tactical asset. Tika extended the party's reach throughout the session. His ability to fly, turn invisible, and shapeshift made him invaluable for exploring high areas the party could not reach, retrieving the [[Zephyr Reliquary]] from an elevated shelf, and spying on the rival party's plans from close range.

Key Contributions

  • Flew up to retrieve the [[Zephyr Reliquary]] from a 30-foot-high shelf, guided by [[Jezebel]]'s wind (Session 1)
  • Looted additional items from high shelves while exploring the prism room (Session 1)
  • Fought the [[Giant Constrictor Snake]] as a raven, dealing 7 piercing damage by pecking its eye (Session 1)
  • Was killed by the snake (paralyzed, then bitten with a critical hit that destroyed the raven form and killed the imp) (Session 1)
  • Resummoned via Lavender's 10-minute Find Familiar ritual (Session 1)
  • Flew invisible to spy on the rival party, reporting their plan to break mirrors and wake the T-Rex (Session 1)

Information Held

What He Told the Party

Information Context Session Reliability
The rival party plans to break mirrors to wake the T-Rex so it kills the party, then steal the idol Scouted the rival party while invisible 1 Confirmed true — rival party did exactly this
The rival party has lost three members and is down to three people Observation during reconnaissance 1 Confirmed by DM
The rival party is nervous and scared Emotional observation during scouting 1 Confirmed by DM

Disposition Toward Each PC

PC Disposition Basis Trajectory

[[Bob]] Amicable Bob joked about taking Tika with him via Dimension Door; general positive banter Stable
Other PCs Neutral — cooperative Followed Lavender's orders to help the party; no direct individual relationships Stable

DM Voice Notes

Portrayal Summary

Tika is a tiny New Jersey demon with more personality than his size suggests. Voice him with a nasal, slightly confrontational accent — "Hey! Meow." He is obedient but sassy, doing what Lavender asks while adding his own commentary. In combat, he is brave to the point of recklessness (pecking out a giant snake's eye as a raven). He should feel like a loyal pet with the attitude of a wise-cracking sidekick.

Unresolved Threads

  • [ ] No significant unresolved threads — Tika's role is fully resolved within the one-shot

Jezebel

[No direct dialogue — communicated through wind]

Overview

Jezebel is [[Lavender]]'s Genie patron, contained within a locket that Lavender found. Their relationship is defined by a deal: Lavender received warlock powers in exchange for eventually freeing Jezebel. During the session, Jezebel communicated by sending wind — guiding Lavender (through [[Tika]]) toward the [[Zephyr Reliquary]] on a high shelf. The Reliquary itself matched the design of Lavender's locket, suggesting a deeper connection between Jezebel and the temple's artifacts.

Appearance & Mannerisms

  • Physical Description: Never directly seen. Exists within Lavender's locket/vessel. Associated with wind and cold air.
  • Notable Mannerisms: Communicates through whistling wind that only Lavender can hear.

[!note] Description Completeness

Jezebel was never physically described or directly voiced. All information comes from Lavender's backstory explanation and the wind-communication scene.

Role in Story

Patron and indirect guide. Jezebel's wind-communication led the party to discover the [[Zephyr Reliquary]], which granted Lavender fire immunity — one of the most tactically important items in the session. Jezebel also provides the Genie's Vessel (Bottled Respite) ability, which the party considered using to hide from poison gas.

Key Contributions

  • Guided [[Tika]] to the [[Zephyr Reliquary]] through wind communication (Session 1)
  • Provides Lavender's warlock powers and Genie's Vessel ability (background/ongoing)

Connections

Relationships to Other Characters

Character Nature of Relationship Details Source
[[Lavender]] Patron/Beneficiary Made a deal: Lavender gets warlock powers, Jezebel eventually gets freed Lavender's backstory

DM Voice Notes

Portrayal Summary

Jezebel has no spoken voice — she is a presence, not a character in the traditional sense. She communicates through environmental effects: cold wind, whistling through corridors, guiding attention to important objects. If she ever speaks directly, it should feel elemental — like the wind itself forming words. She is helpful and invested in Lavender's survival, presumably because Lavender is her ticket to freedom.

Unresolved Threads

  • [ ] Will Lavender fulfill the deal to free Jezebel?
  • [ ] What is Jezebel's connection to the [[Zephyr Reliquary]], which matched her locket's design?
  • [ ] What type of genie is Jezebel? (Wind/air association suggests Djinni)

Last updated after Session 1 (One-shot). Profile compiled from the Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip session transcript.
