Key Growth Moments
MOOR
Moor -- Dragonborn Cleric (Life Domain)
Net Growth +6
Direction Stabilizing
Performance Radar
Grade Report
A
Courage
Went first into every room, tested every trap with his own mace, stood in front of beams to shield others. Never flinched.
A
Teamwork
148 HP healed. Cast Aid, Shield of Faith, Guidance on others constantly. Gave his shield to Lavender. The party's backbone.
A
Moral Integrity
Lawful good from start to finish. Grabbed the Scales of Justice. Cast Command to make enemies flee rather than slaughter them. The cleric of Tyr never wavered.
C
Emotional Growth
Moor is Moor. He didn't open up emotionally, but his actions spoke volumes. The gap between his words and his deeds IS his emotional expression.
A
Problem-Solving
Mirror gambit, pressure plate methodology, gold-piling system, strategic use of Light spell on a rock for the T-Rex room. Brilliant.
A
Character Consistency
Perfectly embodied "few words, big actions" the entire session without a single break in character.
B
MVP Moments
Essential but quiet. His contributions were foundational rather than flashy. The mirror kill and "Run through door" were standout moments.
Trait Snapshot: Before & After
Confidence
+1
7 -- Quiet but assured, immediately took charge of marching order
8 -- Anchored the entire party's survival without hesitation
Trust in Others
+1
5 -- Cooperative but cautious, tested the room before letting others proceed
6 -- Trusted the party enough to delegate tasks and split up
Moral Compass
0
8 -- Lawful good cleric of Tyr, grabbed the Scales of Justice instinctively
8 -- Maintained moral center throughout; cast Command to stop enemies rather than kill
Emotional State
0
6 -- Stoic, functional, "why use many word when few word do trick"
6 -- Same stoic efficiency, but moments of dry warmth toward companions
Goals / Purpose
+1
5 -- Hired mercenary, no personal stakes beyond the job
6 -- Found purpose as the party's protector and decision-maker
Connection to Party
+2
4 -- Professional distance, focused on logistics
6 -- Healed everyone repeatedly, shielded Lavender, guided Opal while blind
Combat Instincts
+1
7 -- Smart and strategic, immediately thought to turn the mirror on the gorilla
8 -- Consistently the first to see tactical solutions, used Turn Undead decisively
Self-Awareness
0
7 -- Knows exactly who he is, plays to his strengths
7 -- No change needed; Moor is already self-possessed
Key Growth Moments
Moment 1: “The Mirror Gambit”
When the gorillons attacked at the temple entrance, Moor immediately asked if the thermal mirror could be turned. He rolled an 18 strength check, angled the beam at the gorilla, and melted it on the spot -- solving the encounter without a single weapon swing.
This set the tone for Moor's entire session: quiet, efficient problem-solving. He didn't grandstand or explain. He just did it. The party immediately started copying his approach, and Moor became the tactical anchor for every room.
Combat Instincts (established at 7), Confidence (established at 7)
Moment 2: “The Pressure Plate Methodologist”
While others wanted to rush forward, Moor systematically tested the pressure plates with his mace, experimented with weight thresholds using the shield, cast Mending when his mace handle was sliced, and devised the gold-piling system that allowed safe passage.
Moor became the party's engineer. His patience -- "When the time comes. When the time comes." -- saved lives. He turned a deadly gauntlet into a solvable puzzle, demonstrating that his few words carry enormous tactical weight.
Problem-Solving anchor, Trust in Others ↑ (delegated tasks to others while he experimented)
Moment 3: “148 Hit Points of Devotion”
Moor healed the party for 148 HP across the session -- every single point of healing the party received. He used Aid to boost three party members, Prayer of Healing after the gas trap, Cure Wounds to bring Aramil back from unconsciousness at -10 HP, Lesser Restoration from the Staff of Healing to cure Opal's blindness, and still had spell slots reserved for emergencies.
Moor never once complained about his healing role. He didn't ask for thanks. He just kept the party alive. When he said "I got him" after Aramil went down, it was the most emotionally revealing moment from a character who speaks in sentence fragments. Connection to Party moved from professional to protective.
Connection to Party ↑ +2, Goals/Purpose ↑ +1
Moment 4: “Run Through Door”
When the party was debating what to do after pulling the swords, Moor cut through the noise: "Run through door. Why use many word when few word do trick?" The DM was stunned. The party was stunned. Everyone ran.
This was Moor distilled to his essence -- and the party loved him for it. His brevity isn't a limitation; it's leadership. In a group of debaters and overthinkers, Moor's clarity was worth more than any spell slot.
Confidence ↑ +1 (comfortable enough to lead through bluntness)
Teacher's Note

Moor, I want you to know that I see what you do when you think no one is watching. I see you testing the pressure plate with your mace before anyone else steps forward. I see you casting Aid on three people and not including yourself. I see you standing behind Opal with Guidance ready the moment she needs it, never asking for credit, never even mentioning it.*

*You healed 148 hit points this session. Every single point of recovery this party received came from your hands, your spells, your staff. And when Aramil was at negative ten, wrapped in a giant constrictor snake, you didn't hesitate -- "I got him." Two words. That is the most Moor sentence in the world, and it might be my favorite thing anyone said all night.*

*What makes you remarkable isn't just your tactical mind -- though that mirror gambit in the first room was genuinely inspired. It's that you lead without needing to be seen leading. "Run through door. Why use many word when few word do trick?" You made an entire table of overthinkers move with three words. That's not stoicism. That's authority.*

*The one thing I'd gently push you on: let them see what's behind the efficiency sometimes. When you healed Opal's blindness, there was a moment -- just a flicker -- where the cleric of Tyr was being kind, not just effective. More of that. The party trusts you with their hit points. I think they'd trust you with a full sentence, too.*

*You are the foundation this party was built on. And foundations don't get enough credit. So here it is: thank you, Moor. Well done.

OPAL
Opal -- Goliath Barbarian
Net Growth +9
Direction Awakening
Performance Radar
Grade Report
A
Courage
Went blind from a thermal beam and kept fighting. Killed the snake with a crit. Volunteered for every physical challenge. Fearless.
A
Teamwork
Helped move mirrors, pushed snake statues with Lavender, held swords with Moor, helped pull ropes in the escape. Always first to say "I can do that."
B
Moral Integrity
Good-hearted throughout, no moral compromises. Didn't have complex ethical choices but stayed true to her nature.
B
Emotional Growth
Going blind forced vulnerability. Accepting help, holding Lavender's hand, acknowledging "Goliath feel calm or angry" -- real emotional range for a low-INT barbarian.
C
Problem-Solving
Followed others' plans more than creating her own. Tried to apply Moor's mirror strategy but rolled a nat 1. Heart's in the right place; brain catches up eventually.
A
Character Consistency
Energetic, big-hearted, and a little bit confused the entire time. Never broke character. "I don't know what that does but cool" is peak Opal.
B
MVP Moments
The blind snake kill was legendary. "I hate mirrors! Hold this mirror!" is an all-time quote. Strong main-character energy when it counted.
Trait Snapshot: Before & After
Confidence
+1
7 -- Enthusiastic, eager to be in front, "I'm happy to be up front"
8 -- Killed the giant snake while blind, never stopped volunteering
Trust in Others
+2
7 -- Immediately delighted by the group, "I'm delighted to be here"
9 -- Held Lavender's hand while blind, let Moor heal her, trusted Bob between her legs
Moral Compass
0
6 -- Good-hearted but uncomplicated, no moral dilemmas faced yet
6 -- Remained good-hearted; flipped off the rival adventurers but didn't hurt anyone unprovoked
Emotional State
-1
8 -- "Constantly energetic despite being intellectually low" -- pure joy
7 -- Took a hit (literally and figuratively) going blind, but recovered her spirit
Goals / Purpose
+1
4 -- "I definitely saw a flier" -- here for fun, no deeper motivation
5 -- Became invested in the team's survival, wanted to get everyone out
Connection to Party
+2
6 -- Friendly to everyone immediately, but surface-level
8 -- Deep bonds formed through adversity -- holding hands with Lavender, fighting alongside Moor
Combat Instincts
+3
5 -- Enthusiastic but untested, "I haven't even gotten to use my great axe"
8 -- Critical hit on the snake while BLIND, resisted grapple with 24 athletics
Self-Awareness
+1
4 -- "I'm a Goliath" is about as deep as self-reflection goes
5 -- Acknowledged her blindness with humor and grace: "Goliath feel calm or angry"
Key Growth Moments
Moment 1: “I Hate Mirrors! Hold This Mirror!”
Opal raged and tried to move the thermal mirror to replicate Moor's tactic, rolling poorly on her first attempt. She screamed "I hate mirrors! Hold this mirror!" in pure frustrated Goliath glory, then with Bob's help and Guidance, finally moved it on a natural 20.
This was Opal learning that enthusiasm alone isn't enough -- sometimes you need help. Accepting Bob crawling between her legs to assist, accepting Guidance from Moor -- Opal went from solo bulldozer to team player in one encounter.
Trust in Others ↑ +1, Combat Instincts ↑ +1
Moment 2: “Fourteen Damage to the Eyes”
Opal rolled a natural 1 trying to move the mirror in the crystalline room and fell directly into the thermal beam. She took 14 damage and went blind for an hour. Her response: "Goliath feel calm or angry."
This was Opal's trial. Blinded in a death trap, she could have panicked. Instead, she accepted help -- Lavender's bandana, Lavender's hand, Moor's medicine check. "Someone's gotta tell me where to go and what to do" is the most vulnerable thing Opal says all session. For a character defined by physical power, losing her sight and accepting dependency was enormous growth.
Emotional State ↓ -1 (processing difficulty), Trust in Others ↑ +1, Connection to Party ↑ +1
Moment 3: “Blind Snake Slayer”
While Aramil lay unconscious at -10 HP, wrapped in the giant constrictor snake, Opal -- still adjusting from her blindness, poisoned, and battered -- rolled a critical hit with her great axe, dealing 30 damage and killing the snake in one blow. "I'm going to chop like a cake, but less delicately."
This was Opal's defining combat moment. Blind or not, poisoned or not, when the party needed her most, she delivered the killing blow. The crit wasn't just lucky dice -- it was a character moment. Opal proved that her strength isn't just physical; it's the willingness to swing when it matters.
Combat Instincts ↑ +2, Confidence ↑ +1
Moment 4: “I Hold Lavender's Hand”
While blind, Opal sat with Lavender. Lavender gave her a pretty bandana for her eyes and booped her on the head. Opal said "This is very nice, thank you" and held her hand while they looted together.
In a session full of combat and puzzles, this small moment of tenderness between a blind Goliath barbarian and a sarcastic Tiefling warlock was the emotional heart. Opal let herself be cared for, which, for someone whose identity is "big and strong," required real vulnerability.
Connection to Party ↑ +1, Trust in Others ↑ +1
Teacher's Note

Opal, you walked into this temple like you walk into everything -- arms open, heart first, ready to be delighted. "I'm delighted to be here. I'm delighted by everyone." And you know what? That delight is your superpower. It's easy to be brave when you're calculating the odds. It's something else entirely to be brave because you're too excited to be scared.*

*But the moment that made me proudest wasn't the critical hit on the snake -- though that was spectacular, and I want you to know that killing a giant constrictor while recovering from blindness is not something most adventurers can claim. No, the moment that mattered most was when you said "Someone's gotta tell me where to go and what to do." That was Opal -- strongest person in the room -- admitting she needed help. And then holding Lavender's hand and saying "This is very nice, thank you."*

*You have a heart the size of your great axe, Opal. The way you immediately adopted Bob ("I'm like, ah" when he was between your legs moving the mirror), the way you called Moor "Dragon" with such affection when he healed your eyes, the way you were genuinely worried about everyone's health during the escape -- you care so much, and you don't even try to hide it.*

*Next time, trust your instincts a little more in puzzles. You see more than you think you do. And keep swinging that axe like you mean it -- because you always do.

ARAMIL
Aramil -- Half-Elf Warlock (Archaeologist)
Net Growth +1
Direction Fracturing
Performance Radar
Grade Report
B
Courage
Talked down a gorillon, stayed in combat with the snake, used Phantasmal Force against the rival mages. Not the bravest, but not a coward.
D
Teamwork
Contributed to the group when it served him, but his hidden agenda undermined everything. Untied ropes when asked, but stole the eye that ended the world.
F
Moral Integrity
Received a direct warning that removing the idol's eye would cause eternal darkness. Did it anyway for personal gain. Fascinating, compelling, and completely damning.
C
Emotional Growth
"Maybe my dad finally noticed me" is heartbreaking, but Aramil never processes that pain -- he just acts on it. No growth, but profound emotional depth.
B
Problem-Solving
Speak with Animals on the gorillon, mural investigation, Phantasmal Force on the rival mages, Locate Object with the rod. Creative and intelligent.
A
Character Consistency
Aramil was exactly who he said he was from the beginning: a desperate academic who would do anything for prestige. The eye theft wasn't a betrayal of character -- it was the fulfillment of it.
B
MVP Moments
The gorillon diplomacy was a highlight. The Phantasmal Force in the finale helped save the party. And the eye theft was, objectively, the most consequential action of the entire session.
Trait Snapshot: Before & After
Confidence
+1
6 -- Presenting as knowledgeable, "adjunct professor emeritus," but clearly compensating
7 -- Successfully talked down a gorillon, investigated murals, contributed tactically
Trust in Others
-1
4 -- Paranoid-looking, secretive about his true motivations from the start
3 -- Secretly stole the golden eye, betraying the entire party and world
Moral Compass
-2
4 -- "Pretty evil," motivated by father's approval and personal prestige
2 -- Stole a cursed artifact knowing it would plunge the world into eternal darkness
Emotional State
0
5 -- Defensive, masks insecurity with academic posturing
5 -- Same defensive posture, but now carrying the weight of a world-ending secret
Goals / Purpose
+2
7 -- Clear personal agenda from the start: artifacts, prestige, father's approval
9 -- Achieved his goal at any cost. "If it gets me reinstated at the GYL Community College"
Connection to Party
-1
4 -- Tolerated companions, focused on his own agenda
3 -- Used the party as cover for his theft, endangered everyone
Combat Instincts
+1
5 -- Capable but not dominant, missed several Eldritch Blasts
6 -- Contributed tactically with Phantasmal Force in the finale, spoke with animals creatively
Self-Awareness
+1
6 -- Knows exactly what he is: a washed-up academic chasing relevance
7 -- Made a fully conscious, eyes-open choice to steal the eye. No self-deception.
Key Growth Moments
Moment 1: “Brother Gorillon”
When the gorillon was pacing outside the temple, Aramil used his Speak with Animals ability to intimidate it: "You just saw a lover boy get blasted by this, right? You want that to happen to you? Think about your wife and kids." He rolled a 22 on intimidation, and the beast fled.
This was Aramil at his best -- clever, creative, using words instead of force. It showed what he could be: a team player who solves problems with intelligence and charm. It also foreshadowed his darker tendencies -- even his diplomacy is built on threats.
Combat Instincts ↑ +1, Confidence ↑ +1
Moment 2: “Definitely My Choice to Leave”
During the character introduction break, Aramil revealed his backstory: "I recently took a sabbatical from my position at the Royal Community College... And it was definitely my choice to leave." Then, after Lavender immediately said "So you're fired," he responded: "I am adjunct professor emeritus. Thank you."
This established Aramil's core wound: he's a failure seeking redemption through artifacts, and the facade is paper-thin. "Maybe my dad finally noticed me" was said with practiced casualness that fooled no one. Everything Aramil does in this session traces back to this desperate need.
Self-Awareness (established at 6), Goals/Purpose (established at 7)
Moment 3: “The Mural Warning”
Aramil investigated the temple murals and discovered that the snake idol was connected to the sun -- removing it might "throw the land into eternal darkness." His response: "Cool. I'm cool with that."
This is the moral fracture point. Aramil received explicit warning about the consequences of his actions and chose to proceed anyway. "If it gets me reinstated at the GYL Community College, then we're okay" reveals that his personal ambition outweighs literally the fate of the world. This isn't ignorance. It's choice.
Moral Compass ↓ -2, Goals/Purpose ↑ +2
Moment 4: “The Sleight of Hand”
As the party loaded into the canoes to leave, Aramil rolled an 18 on Sleight of Hand to secretly pluck the golden eye from the snake idol. No one saw him do it. The sun went out. Eternal darkness fell on Faerun.
This was the culmination of everything Aramil is: brilliant, selfish, desperate, and fully aware of what he's doing. He didn't steal the eye out of ignorance or impulse. He planned it. He knew the consequences. He did it anyway. "I will put it in my office in the community college" -- spoken like a man who just ended the world for a tenure-track position.
Trust in Others ↓ -1, Connection to Party ↓ -1, Self-Awareness ↑ +1
Teacher's Note

Aramil. I've been thinking about what to say to you, and I keep coming back to that moment in the hallway when you told the others about your "sabbatical." "It was definitely my choice to leave." The way you said it -- practiced, defensive, daring anyone to challenge the lie. And then, quieter: "Maybe my dad finally noticed me."*

*You are, without question, the most intelligent person in this party. The gorillon diplomacy was masterful. "Think about your wife and kids" -- said in the beast's own language, with advantage, rolling a 22. That's not just cleverness. That's someone who understands how others think and what they fear. You could use that gift to build something extraordinary.*

*Instead, you used it to steal a golden eye from a snake idol and plunge an entire continent into eternal darkness.*

*I'm not going to pretend I'm not disappointed. You read the murals. You understood the warning. You looked at the consequences -- the undead rising, the sun going dark, the world falling apart -- and you said "Cool. I'm cool with that." Because a community college might give you your office back.*

*But here's what I need you to hear: the fact that you knew what you were doing makes you more interesting, not less. You didn't steal that eye out of ignorance. You stole it because the hole your father left in you is so deep that you'd fill it with the world's light if you could. That's not evil. That's grief wearing a fedora.*

*I hope you find what you're looking for, Aramil. I suspect the eye isn't it. But the search? The search is one of the most compelling stories at this table. Keep searching. Just... maybe check the murals next time before you commit to the apocalypse.

LAVENDER
Lavender -- Tiefling Warlock (Genie/Chain Pact)
Net Growth +14
Direction Ascending
Performance Radar
Grade Report
A
Courage
Walked through thermal beams with fire immunity to move snake statues. Cleared poison gas twice with Gust of Wind. Shielded Opal through dangerous areas with her own body.
A
Teamwork
Gave Moor a shield, held Opal's hand while blind, offered Genie Vessel as emergency escape for the whole party, sent Tika to scout and loot for others.
B
Moral Integrity
Consistent moral center. Suggested befriending the rival team (overruled). No compromises, no betrayals. Practical without being ruthless.
A
Emotional Growth
From nervous newcomer to the session's emotional anchor. The Opal moment was genuine tenderness. Genuine stress during the escape. Real joy at surviving. Full emotional range.
A
Problem-Solving
Gust of Wind (twice), Hellish Rebuke chain reaction, fire immunity walkthrough, Tika as invisible scout, Zephyr Reliquary decision. Session MVP for a reason.
A
Character Consistency
Sarcastic, practical, warm-hearted, and increasingly confident from start to finish. Never broke character. "If the men find out we can shapeshift" is peak Lavender.
A
MVP Moments
Session MVP with 8.4 weighted score. Multiple decisive contributions: Gust of Wind, Hellish Rebuke, fire immunity strategy, Tika scouting, Opal friendship. She was the session's engine.
Trait Snapshot: Before & After
Confidence
+2
6 -- Cheerful but self-deprecating: "I don't know how to play D&D"
8 -- Session MVP, solved multiple encounters, made decisive calls
Trust in Others
+2
6 -- Friendly and cooperative from the start, immediately engaging
8 -- Trusted the party with her Genie Vessel escape plan, relied on Opal for strength
Moral Compass
0
6 -- Practical and deal-oriented (made a deal with a genie), but good-hearted
6 -- Maintained her practicality; suggested making friends with rivals (overruled) but didn't push it
Emotional State
+1
6 -- Optimistic and sarcastic in equal measure, emotionally steady
7 -- Showed genuine tenderness with Opal, genuine stress during the escape, real joy at surviving
Goals / Purpose
+2
5 -- Looking for the golden snake, aligned with the group mission
7 -- Became the party's problem-solver and strategist, found personal purpose beyond loot
Connection to Party
+3
5 -- Sociable but new to everyone
8 -- Booped Opal on the head, held her hand, shielded her through beams, offered Genie Vessel to save everyone
Combat Instincts
+2
5 -- Eldritch Blast and familiar tactics, competent but untested
7 -- Gust of Wind to disperse gas, Hellish Rebuke killing two baboons, fire immunity walkthrough, Tika as scout
Self-Awareness
+2
5 -- "I don't know how to play D&D" was genuine uncertainty
7 -- Recognized her value: "I'm just so impressed that we actually did it"
Key Growth Moments
Moment 1: “If the Men Find Out We Can Shapeshift”
When discussing Tika's ability to transform into different animals, Lavender dropped the quote of the session: "If the men find out we can shapeshift, they're going to tell the church." The entire table erupted.
This wasn't just comedy -- it established Lavender as someone who thinks laterally, sees absurd connections, and isn't afraid to be the funniest person in the room. It was also the moment Lavender went from "new player figuring things out" to "essential party member." Confidence shifted visibly.
Confidence ↑ +1, Self-Awareness ↑ +1
Moment 2: “The Zephyr Reliquary Decision”
Tika found the Zephyr Reliquary -- a powerful artifact that granted fire immunity but was cooling the entire room. Removing it would cause rising heat damage for everyone. Lavender chose to take it: "If she says do it, I'm going to do it. She's my butt." Her trust in Jezebel, her genie patron, was absolute.
This was Lavender's first major strategic decision, and it shaped the rest of the session. Her fire immunity let her walk through thermal beams, move snake statues, and shield Opal through dangerous areas. One decision, cascading consequences. It also showed her bond with her patron is genuine -- not transactional, but relational.
Goals/Purpose ↑ +1, Combat Instincts ↑ +1
Moment 3: “I Boop You on the Head”
When Opal went blind, Lavender gave her a bandana for her eyes, booped her on the head, and held her hand while they looted together. "I hold your hand. Okay. I hold Lavender's hand. And then I'm like, this is very nice, thank you."
Lavender's sarcasm and practicality could have made her distant. Instead, she became the party's emotional center -- the one who responded to Opal's vulnerability with gentle kindness. This moment bonded two characters who had just met and created the session's most tender scene.
Connection to Party ↑ +2, Emotional State ↑ +1
Moment 4: “Gust of Wind Saves the Party”
After the entire party took 14 poison damage from noxious gas, Lavender cast Gust of Wind to disperse it -- then used the same spell again later to clear the gas a second time before walking through. She also used Hellish Rebuke to incinerate two baboons that attacked her, with the volatile heat in the room spreading the fire.
This was Lavender's tactical awakening. She went from "I don't know how to play D&D" to "I have the exact right spell for this situation and I'm using it." Her spell selection, familiar management, and fire immunity made her the session's MVP (8.4 weighted score), and she earned it through creative, consistent problem-solving.
Combat Instincts ↑ +1, Confidence ↑ +1, Goals/Purpose ↑ +1
Teacher's Note

Lavender, you started this session by saying "I don't know how to play D&D." I want you to remember that, because by the end of this session, you were the MVP, and it wasn't even close.*

*What happened in between was one of the most satisfying character arcs I've ever witnessed in a single session. You went from uncertain and deferring to decisive, creative, and essential. When the poison gas hit and everyone was panicking, you cast Gust of Wind. When the baboons attacked, you Hellish Rebuked one so hard the fire spread to its neighbor. When the snake statues needed moving and everyone else would burn, you said "I'll do it" -- because you were the only one who could.*

*But the thing that makes you special isn't the spells. It's the way you treated Opal when she went blind. You gave her a bandana. You booped her on the head. You held her hand. In a session full of thermal beams and T-Rexes and poison gas, you stopped to be kind to someone who was scared. That's not tactics. That's character.*

*"If the men find out we can shapeshift, they're going to tell the church." That line made an entire table lose it, and it's the perfect distillation of who you are: sharp, surprising, a little bit wicked, and absolutely delightful. Your relationship with Tika is genuine ("She's my butt"), your trust in Jezebel is beautiful, and your willingness to offer the Genie Vessel to save the whole party -- knowing it was your one-per-day escape -- shows a generosity that your sarcasm tries very hard to hide.*

*You're not hiding anymore, Lavender. And the table is better for it.

BOB
Bob -- Halfling Rogue
Net Growth +13
Direction Ascending
Performance Radar
Grade Report
A
Courage
Screamed down a hallway to draw baboons away. Hung upside-down over a sleeping T-Rex. Took a fireball mid-flight and didn't drop the idol.
B
Teamwork
Searched for loot for other party members. Drew baboons away. Offered Dimension Door strategy. But still fundamentally a lone operator who works best solo.
C
Moral Integrity
Neither heroic nor villainous. Here for the money, got the money, didn't betray anyone. Neutral isn't exciting, but it's honest.
B
Emotional Growth
From maximum distance ("I'm, uh... Bob") to genuine camaraderie ("I was going to say we were buddies"). The hood came off, metaphorically.
A
Problem-Solving
Spider-wall crossing solution, ceiling-escape from baboons, Indiana Jones idol swap, Batman extraction. Consistently the cleverest person in the room.
A
Character Consistency
A rogue who acts like a rogue: sneaky, clever, self-interested but surprisingly reliable when it counts. Every action was perfectly in character.
A
MVP Moments
The idol extraction was the single most critical action of the session. Without Bob, the mission fails. Period.
Trait Snapshot: Before & After
Confidence
+2
6 -- Quietly competent, comfortable in the shadows: "I'm just here for the loot, baby"
8 -- Batman cape fly-by to grab the idol, ceiling-walking escape, absolute clutch
Trust in Others
+2
4 -- Sarcastic distance, hood pulled deep, identity obscured
6 -- Worked with Opal on the mirror, offered Dimension Door strategy, trusted the party to cover him
Moral Compass
0
5 -- Neutral, mercenary, here for profit
5 -- Remained neutral. Got the loot, didn't betray anyone, didn't go out of his way to help strangers either
Emotional State
+1
5 -- Guarded, hides behind sarcasm: "I'm, uh... Married."
6 -- Showed genuine excitement during the idol grab, real fear during the fireball
Goals / Purpose
+2
6 -- Clear mission: loot everything that isn't nailed down
8 -- Became the party's extraction specialist, found key items for others, secured the primary objective
Connection to Party
+3
3 -- "I'm, uh... Bob." Maximum distance from everyone
6 -- Searched for loot for Shannon specifically, drew baboons away to save the group, supported Opal
Combat Instincts
+2
6 -- Competent with sneak attack, good positioning instincts
8 -- Ceiling-walking distraction play, clutch sneak attacks, Sleight of Hand idol swap, Batman extraction
Self-Awareness
+1
6 -- Knows exactly what he is: a thief in the shadows
7 -- Stepped into the spotlight when needed, then slid back to the shadows. Knows when to be seen.
Key Growth Moments
Moment 1: “How Many Squirtons in a Gusher?”
In the opening moments, as the DM described the currency system, Bob delivered the session's first big laugh: "And how many squirtons in a gusher?" It brought the house down and established Bob's comedic timing.
This early moment established Bob as the party's comic relief -- but a specific kind: the deadpan observer who says the thing everyone is thinking. It also showed that beneath the deep hood and sarcastic distance, Bob is paying attention to everything.
Self-Awareness (established at 6)
Moment 2: “Big, Big, Step, Step”
When the party couldn't figure out how to cross the pressure plates, Bob proposed the solution: walk on the walls with Spider Slippers while everyone else pairs up. "Pull out my crayons. This is you. Big. Big, big, step, step." Drawing it out for Opal like a kindergarten teacher.
Bob went from "secluded" to "the person explaining the plan." His willingness to break his cool-guy persona to literally draw diagrams for his teammates showed unexpected patience and investment in the group's success.
Connection to Party ↑ +1, Trust in Others ↑ +1
Moment 3: “Screaming Down the Hallway”
When baboons swarmed the crystal room, Bob looked at the party, told them to keep working on the puzzle, and then ran screaming down the hallway to draw the blind baboons away. He led them to the spike room, then climbed up to the ceiling and went silent, watching them impale themselves on the pressure plate traps. Stealth roll: 27.
This was Bob's most selfless moment. The loot guy, the "I'm just here for the money" guy, voluntarily drew a pack of feral baboons toward himself so the rest of the party could solve a puzzle. Then he outsmarted them with Spider Slippers and stealth. It was brave, creative, and generous -- three things Bob would deny being.
Connection to Party ↑ +2, Courage ↑ +1, Combat Instincts ↑ +1
Moment 4: “The Batman Extraction”
Bob climbed the wall in total darkness, hung from the archway above a sleeping T-Rex, Sleight of Handed the golden idol into his bag (roll: 22), then leapt off holding both sides of his Cloak of the Bat, flying out into the dark. A fireball hit him mid-flight. He saved for half damage. He didn't drop the idol.
This was the climax of the entire session, and Bob was the one who did it. The secluded halfling who started the session hiding in his hood ended it flying over a T-Rex with a world-changing artifact in his bag. The DM said the entire party surviving was unprecedented. Bob made it possible.
Confidence ↑ +2, Combat Instincts ↑ +1, Goals/Purpose ↑ +2
Teacher's Note

Bob. Or should I say, "just Bob." The kid who showed up on the first day with his hood pulled so deep you could barely see his eyes, wrote his own name on his hand in case he forgot it, and told us he was "just here for the loot, baby."*

*And then you screamed down a hallway to draw a pack of feral baboons away from your friends.*

*That's the thing about you, Bob. You perform distance -- the hood, the sarcasm, the minimal information. "I'm, uh... Married." "This fucking guy." You want everyone to think you're an island. And then you spend your search time finding a Belt of Stone Giant Strength for Shannon and a cape for Kyle, and you draw a stick-figure diagram on the ground to explain your Spider Slipper plan to Opal because she didn't understand the words.*

*The Batman moment over the T-Rex was objectively the highlight of the session, and you earned it. Climbing in total darkness, hanging by your legs, Sleight of Hand 22 on the idol, flying out on a cape while a fireball explodes behind you. That is the kind of moment that D&D was built for. And it happened because a halfling rogue who said he was "just here for the loot" decided to be the one who grabbed it.*

*But what I'll remember most is the baboon hallway. You looked at the party and said "start screaming and making as much noise as I can running down the hallway." Then stealth 27, up the ceiling, silent. That's not a thief. That's someone who cares more than he'd ever admit. The night, indeed, Bob. The night indeed.

Per-Pair Interaction Analysis

Moor ↔ Lavender

Interaction Metrics

  • Direct Exchanges: 22
  • "Yes, And" Moments: 4 (Coordinating shield/mace pressure plate tests)
  • Combat Assists: 3 (Moor healing Lavender; Shield of Faith)
  • Shared Banter / Humor: 2 (Friendly fire incident)

Interaction Examples

  • Best "Yes, And" Moment: Moor suggests using weights on pressure plates; Lavender immediately offers her heavy shield to help find the "threshold."
  • Combat Synergy Example: Moor casts Shield of Faith on Lavender while she moves to distract the T-Rex.
  • Notable Disagreement: Minor OOC/IC confusion when Lavender accidentally hits Moor with an Eldritch Blast (Friendly Fire).

Chemistry Type: The Brain Trust / Chaos Partners

Chemistry Score: 9/10

Justification: These two were the primary mechanical problem solvers for the puzzles. Their dialogue was constant, focusing on the "how-to" of the mirrors and pressure plates.

Opal ↔ Lavender

Interaction Metrics

  • Direct Exchanges: 18
  • "Yes, And" Moments: 3 (The bandana/blindness scene)
  • Emotional Moments Shared: 2

Interaction Examples

  • Best "Yes, And": Lavender suggests using her fire immunity to guide the blind Opal through the light beams.

Chemistry Type: Odd Couple / Loyal Companions

Chemistry Score: 9/10

Justification: This pair provided the strongest emotional heart of the session. The "Big Sister/Little Sister" dynamic between the tiny Tiefling and the massive Goliath was a highlight.

Interaction Metrics

  • "Yes, And" Moments: 2 (Lifting the gate/ropes)
  • Combat Assists: 4 (Moor healing/Lesser Restoration)

Interaction Examples

  • Notable Disagreement: Moor tries to manage Opal's rage usage/strength checks.

Chemistry Type: Battle Siblings

Chemistry Score: 8/10

Justification: They operated as the "front line" and "the muscle," coordinating physical tasks and health management.

Bob ↔ Aramil

Interaction Metrics

  • "Yes, And" Moments: 2 (The Home Alone escape plan)

Interaction Examples

Justification: Lowest direct interaction count. They mostly operated independently (Bob sneaking/Aramil investigating), though they synchronized well for the final sprint.

Interaction Examples

Chemistry Type: Comic Relief Duo

Chemistry Score: 7/10

Justification: Their interactions were almost entirely based on their extreme size difference (Goliath vs. Halfling), providing consistent table laughs.

Consensus Patterns Analysis

Decision Points

# Decision Who Led Who Supported Who Resisted Who Was Silent Resolution Time
1 Closing the Door Moor Aramil Lavender Bob/Opal Consensus Quick
2 Pressure Plates Moor Lavender None Bob/Aramil Experiment Moderate
3 Moving Statue 1 Lavender Opal None Bob/Moor Consensus Quick
4 Fighting Snake Opal Aramil Moor Bob/Lavender Combat Quick
5 Dealing with T-Rex Aramil Bob None Moor/Opal Stealth/Fly Moderate
6 The Skill Challenge Moor/DM All None None Coordination Long

Decision-Making Roles

Player Character Times Led Times Supported Times Resisted Times Silent Influence Score
Kyle 1 Moor 3 2 1 0 9/10
Kelsey Lavender 2 3 1 0 8/10
Shannon Opal 1 4 0 1 6/10
Kyle 2 Aramil 1 2 0 3 5/10
Josh Bob 0 3 0 3 4/10

Consensus Health Score: 8.5 / 10

The party is highly functional. Moor often initiates the plan, but Lavender almost always refines it. There is very little "alpha-gaming," as players generally wait for others to finish their "bits" or tasks.

Group Dynamics Findings

Party Social Architecture

  • Core Bond: Moor ↔ Lavender ↔ Opal. This triangle formed the functional engine of the session, handling healing, puzzles, and front-line combat.
  • Social Connector: Lavender. She had meaningful interactions with every member, notably bridging the gap between the "grumpy" front-liners and the "sneaky" back-liners.
  • Dramatic Engine: Moor ↔ Lavender. Their shared spellcaster logic and the friendly-fire incident provided the most moment-to-moment momentum.
  • Comedy Team: Opal ↔ Bob. The physical comedy of the Goliath and the Halfling was a recurring theme.

Interaction Health Score: 8.0 / 10

A very healthy session. While Aramil and Bob were slightly more isolated (focusing on their individual stealth/investigation tasks), the "Skill Challenge" at the end forced a total party integration that fixed any early-session silences.

Key Observations

  1. Support Harmony: Moor (the Cleric) and Lavender (the Warlock) show zero competition for the "leader" role; they build on each other's ideas effectively.
  2. Physicality as RP: Opal and Bob used their character sizes (Goliath vs. Halfling) to dictate their chemistry more than their backstories.
  3. The "Skill Challenge" Glue: The high-stress escape at the end was the first time Bob and Aramil truly integrated with the main social group, suggesting they thrive under "clutch" conditions.

    4. Opportunities for Deeper Chemistry

    • Aramil ↔ Moor: As the two "older" or more "professional" characters (Professor vs. Cleric), they have room for a "clash of ideologies" or a "Brain Trust" expansion.
    • Bob ↔ Lavender: Both have high sarcasm/humor potential. A scene where they plan a heist or a prank could solidify their "Chaos Partner" status.

Psychological Analysis

Personality

Character Personality Analyses — Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip

Analysis based on a single session transcript. All assessments are provisional and would benefit from multiple sessions of data.

Moor — Personality Analysis

Dragonborn Cleric (Life Domain) | Played by Kyle 1

MYERS-BRIGGS TYPE INDICATOR: ISTJ

"The Logistician" — Quietly efficient, methodical, duty-bound

Dimension Breakdown:

Extraversion (E) vs. Introversion (I): I

Assessment: Clear I

Evidence:

  • Moor speaks in short, functional bursts throughout the session. His dialogue is almost exclusively task-oriented — he does not engage in banter, storytelling, or social elaboration. When others are joking about squirtons and cashmere hats, Moor is asking about carrying capacity and inventory mechanics.
  • He processes internally before acting. When examining the pressure plates, he methodically tests them with his mace rather than talking through his plan first. He announces conclusions, not thought processes: "We should make the decision when I run out of spots."

Key moment: "Run through door. Why use many word when few word do trick?" — This is not just a joke; it is a genuine behavioral philosophy. Moor conserves verbal energy in a way that is textbook introversion.

Sensing (S) vs. Intuition (N): S

Assessment: Clear S

Evidence:

  • Moor focuses relentlessly on concrete, immediate details: AC values, weight thresholds for pressure plates, the exact mechanics of ropes and counterweights. When the party is speculating about the mural symbolism, Moor is asking "Can we access the stone weights?" and "How are these staying?"
  • His problem-solving is sequential and hands-on. He tests the pressure plates one at a time with his mace, observes the result, then tests the next variable. He never leaps to theoretical conclusions.

Key moment: When the party discovers pressure plates, Moor immediately takes out his mace and physically pushes down on a tile — sensory, empirical testing rather than theorizing.

Thinking (T) vs. Feeling (F): T

Assessment: Moderate T (with a notable F undercurrent through healing)

Evidence:

  • Decision-making is consistently logic-driven. When Opal goes blind, Moor's response is not emotional comfort but a medicine check to assess duration and a cost-benefit analysis of whether to use a spell slot: "I don't want to use my only other second-level spell."
  • He allocates healing resources with strategic precision, tracking everyone's HP and distributing efficiently: "How's everybody's health? 17 out of 21?" He heals Aramil to exactly the amount needed, wastes nothing.
  • However, his T is tempered by genuine caretaking. He cast Aid on three party members proactively, and he consistently positions himself behind allies to provide Guidance and healing. The healing is a T expression of duty, not an F expression of emotional connection.

Key moment: "Cry. I'm going to cast Aid on you three. Not you." — The joke masks a strategic allocation of a limited resource. He chose the three who needed it most without sentiment.

Judging (J) vs. Perceiving (P): J

Assessment: Clear J

Evidence:

  • Moor is the party's procedural backbone. He establishes marching order ("I'll go first now that I have an 18 AC"), he pushes for systematic approaches to the pressure plates (synchronized stepping, gold piling), and he consistently advocates for moving forward rather than lingering.
  • He shows visible frustration with the party's indecisiveness. When others are debating, he cuts through: "From here on out, we're sprinting everywhere." He wants closure and movement, not open-ended exploration.

Key moment: "I think we've given up on this puzzle for right now. Let's get out of here. We have to lay the land."

MBTI Summary:

Moor's ISTJ manifests as a stoic, efficient protector who leads through competence rather than charisma. Unlike a stereotypical cleric, his healing is tactical — a logistical function, not an emotional one. He is the party member who makes sure the system works: testing traps, tracking resources, establishing order. His few-words philosophy is not shyness but efficiency — he has weighed the cost of speech against its utility and found most of it wanting.

ENNEAGRAM: Type 1 — "The Reformer"

Wing: 1w9

Core Motivation: To do the right thing correctly and efficiently — to fulfill duty without waste, error, or unnecessary complication. Moor does not seek glory; he seeks the satisfaction of a job properly executed.

Core Fear: Chaos, waste, and moral failure. Being unable to protect those in his charge due to poor preparation or undisciplined decision-making.

Evidence for Type 1:

  • Moor's immediate grab for the Scales of Justice (a symbol of Tyr, god of justice) is instinctive — "I grab it." He does not debate whether to take a lawful good artifact. Of course he takes it. It is an extension of his identity.
  • His systematic approach to every problem (testing plates one at a time, gold-piling in synchronized pairs, casting Light on a rock as contingency) reflects the Type 1 drive for correctness and thoroughness.
  • When Lavender accidentally hits him with Eldritch Blast ("five friendly fire damage"), Moor does not react emotionally. He absorbs it, asks a clarifying mechanics question, and moves on. A Type 1 controls their anger through discipline.

Wing Influence (1w9):

The w9 wing gives Moor his calm, withdrawn quality. He is not a fiery reformer lecturing others on righteousness — he is a quiet one, leading by example and action rather than sermon. His stoicism is the 9-wing's desire for inner peace merged with the 1's drive for correctness.

Integration (Growth) Direction — Type 7:

In moments of security, Moor shows flickers of Type 7 playfulness. His "Why use many word when few word do trick?" bit is genuinely funny, delivered with deadpan timing. His joke about laying hands "on her butt" shows he can be lighthearted when the pressure drops. These moments are rare but genuine.

Disintegration (Stress) Direction — Type 4:

Under stress, Moor becomes emotionally withdrawn. When the gas hits the whole party for 14 damage each and the situation spirals, his first word is "Cry." It is a joke, but it reveals the Type 1's stress response: feeling overwhelmed by the gap between how things should be and how they are.

Enneagram Summary:

Moor is a disciplined, duty-bound 1w9 whose inner critic drives him to optimize every decision. His growth edge is allowing himself to enjoy the adventure rather than merely surviving it. The tension between his rigid efficiency and the party's chaotic energy is the central drama of his character — he loves these fools, but they make his job very hard.

BIG FIVE (OCEAN) PERSONALITY SCORES

Trait Score (1-10) Label Evidence
Openness to Experience 3 Low Moor shows almost no interest in abstract exploration. He ignores murals, does not speculate on lore, and has no curiosity about the temple's history. His focus is entirely practical: "Can we access the stone weights?" He does not wonder what the murals mean; he wants to know what the mechanisms do.
Conscientiousness 9 Very High Moor is the most organized party member by a wide margin. He tracks HP across the group, systematically tests traps, establishes marching order, and allocates healing resources with spreadsheet-like precision. He cast Mending on his broken mace handle — he fixes things, he does not leave loose ends.
Extraversion 2 Very Low Moor speaks less than any other character and almost never initiates social interaction. His dialogue is functional and brief. He does not seek attention, does not tell stories about himself, and does not engage in the party's frequent banter.
Agreeableness 6 Average-High Moor is not warm, but he is deeply cooperative and protective. He heals everyone, gives Guidance freely, casts Shield of Faith on Lavender, and takes point as tank without complaint. His agreeableness is expressed through actions, not words. He pushed back on Opal's shield idea politely: "We should make the decision when I run out of spots."
Neuroticism 3 Low Moor remains calm under significant pressure. When the gas hits, when Opal goes blind, when the T-Rex stirs — his response is always measured and tactical. He does not panic, raise his voice, or express anxiety. "I'm going to cast Light on a rock nearby" is the response of a man with a plan.

OCEAN Profile Visualization:

```

Openness: [███───────] 3/10

Conscientiousness: [█████████─] 9/10

Extraversion: [██────────] 2/10

Agreeableness: [██████────] 6/10

Neuroticism: [███───────] 3/10

```

Profile Interpretation:

This combination — very low O, very high C, very low E, moderate-high A, low N — produces the classic "quiet competence" archetype. Moor is the person you want next to you in a crisis: unshakeable, methodical, and entirely focused on keeping everyone alive. The low openness combined with high conscientiousness means he will always choose the proven solution over the creative one. The moderate agreeableness prevents him from being cold — he cares, he just shows it through doing rather than saying.

BEHAVIORAL PATTERNS

Decision-Making Style

Fast, independent, empirical. Moor decides by testing and observing. He does not poll the group or debate options — he pushes a plate, watches the result, and adjusts. When decisions are needed, he either acts unilaterally ("I grab it") or issues brief directives ("Run through door").

Evidence: The pressure plate sequence — Moor tested one side, observed the spike mechanism, then systematically tested pairs, timing, and weight thresholds before committing the group to a crossing strategy.

Conflict Response

De-escalation through competence. Moor does not engage in interpersonal conflict at all during the session. When Lavender accidentally blasts him with Eldritch Blast, his response is purely mechanical: "That's not plus anything else?" He absorbs the damage and moves on without commentary.

Evidence: The friendly fire moment — five force damage, zero emotional response.

Leadership Style

Servant leadership. Moor leads from behind, providing support (healing, Guidance, Shield of Faith) while others take the spotlight. He makes strategic suggestions but does not demand compliance. He volunteers for the riskiest positions ("I'll go first") without making it a statement.

Evidence: During the escape sequence, Moor readied Command to stop the rival adventurers, cast Light for the team's visibility, and positioned himself to support — all without being asked.

Stress Indicators

Moor's stress manifests as increased brevity and directional urgency. When the situation deteriorates, his sentences get shorter and more imperative: "Let's go." "From here on out, we're sprinting everywhere." "I think it's time to go."

Evidence: Contrast his early-session engagement (explaining mechanics, testing things) with his late-session urgency — by the end, he is almost entirely issuing one-line commands.

Attachment Style (Interpersonal)

Dismissive-avoidant with secure tendencies under pressure. Moor does not form visible emotional bonds during the session, but he protects everyone equally and reliably. He shows attachment through action (healing, shielding) rather than verbal affirmation.

Evidence: He healed Aramil from -10 HP without comment or expectation of gratitude. The attachment is duty-bound rather than emotionally expressive.

FICTIONAL CHARACTER COMPARISON

"Moor is the Geralt of Rivia of the party."

Comparison: Geralt from The Witcher

Why this comparison works:

Both are laconic professionals who communicate in grunts and short phrases while being far more capable and caring than they let on. Geralt's famous "Hmm" energy mirrors Moor's "few word do trick" philosophy. Both are protectors by trade who frame their caretaking as pragmatic obligation rather than emotional investment. Both prefer to solve problems through direct action rather than discussion, and both serve as the calm center of a chaotic ensemble. Moor healing the party while barely speaking is pure Geralt energy — "I don't like you, but I'll make sure you don't die."

Where the comparison breaks down:

Geralt has a sardonic wit and occasional philosophical depth that Moor does not display in this session. Moor is more purely functional — Geralt would have had something to say about the murals. Moor also lacks Geralt's brooding quality; he is not conflicted about his role, just committed to executing it.

Opal — Personality Analysis

Goliath Barbarian | Played by Shannon

MYERS-BRIGGS TYPE INDICATOR: ESFP

"The Entertainer" — Spontaneous, energetic, lives in the present moment

Dimension Breakdown:

Extraversion (E) vs. Introversion (I): E

Assessment: Clear E

Evidence:

  • Opal is the most verbally active character in the session. She comments on everything, reacts audibly to every event, and keeps up a running commentary throughout: "Opal. She's a Goliath. Barbarian. She hewed and jacked." Her energy is directed outward constantly.
  • She draws energy from group interaction. When she goes blind, her primary concern is social: "Someone's gotta tell me where to go and what to do." She holds Lavender's hand. She does not withdraw into herself — she reaches out.

Key moment: "I'm delighted to be here. I'm delighted by everyone." — Opal's default state is social enthusiasm.

Sensing (S) vs. Intuition (N): S

Assessment: Clear S

Evidence:

  • Opal is deeply grounded in the physical and immediate. Her first question upon landing in Chult is "Do we smell dinosaurs?" — sensory, concrete, present-tense. She does not wonder about the temple's history or the idol's significance; she wants to know what she can see, smell, and hit.
  • Her problem-solving is physical and direct. When the mirror needs moving, she rages and pushes. When the snake appears, she wants to "just chop the snake." Theoretical approaches do not occur to her.

Key moment: "I hate mirrors! Hold this mirror!" — Opal processes the world through direct physical interaction with it.

Thinking (T) vs. Feeling (F): F

Assessment: Moderate F

Evidence:

  • Opal leads with emotional responses throughout the session. When she goes blind, she announces "I'm going to cry about how I'm blind" (then clarifies she is fine, because Goliaths feel "calm or angry"). When Lavender gives her a bandana, she says "Oh my gosh, that's so nice."
  • She names her emotions readily and wears them visibly. She expresses delight, frustration, fear, and excitement without filtering. Her decision-making is impulse-driven rather than analytical.
  • However, she is not conflict-averse. She is happy to charge into combat and does not shy away from confrontation — her Feeling is more about emotional expressiveness than about maintaining harmony.

Key moment: "I hold Lavender's hand. And then I'm like, this is very nice, thank you." — Physical and emotional openness in a moment of vulnerability.

Judging (J) vs. Perceiving (P): P

Assessment: Clear P

Evidence:

  • Opal is reactive and spontaneous. She does not plan ahead — she responds to stimuli as they appear. When she sees the obsidian rock: "I go get it. I want it." No cost-benefit analysis, no strategy, just impulse.
  • She adapts fluidly to changing circumstances. Going blind does not shut her down; she immediately pivots to asking others for help and continues contributing. When the mirror-push fails, she rages without hesitation.

Key moment: "What if I just chop the snake?" — The P response to complexity: cut through it, literally.

MBTI Summary:

Opal's ESFP is a joyful, kinetic force that experiences the world through action and reaction. She is the party's emotional weather — when she is delighted, the energy is high; when she is frustrated, it is palpable. Her barbarian class is a perfect expression of her type: she does not plan, she does not theorize, she engages. The combination of high sensory awareness and emotional expressiveness makes her the most "present" character in the party — she is always fully here, fully now.

ENNEAGRAM: Type 7 — "The Enthusiast"

Wing: 7w8

Core Motivation: To experience everything, miss nothing, and stay engaged. Opal's greatest joy is encountering something new and diving headfirst into it. She does not want treasure for its own sake — she wants the thrill of finding it.

Core Fear: Being stuck, bored, or left out. When she goes blind, her fear is not pain but exclusion: "You guys, if you find any good loot, let a girl know, because she can't see."

Evidence for Type 7:

  • Opal's reaction to every new discovery is enthusiastic engagement. Dinosaur smells? "Do we smell dinosaurs?" Pressure plates? "I want to do it." Snake fight? "I'm going to just chop the snake." New tattoo? "I don't know what that does but cool." She says yes to everything.
  • She reframes negatives as positives. Going blind becomes an adventure, not a tragedy. She finds humor in danger: "I didn't get my rock, but that's okay."
  • Her attention bounces between stimuli — she moves from mirror to loot to hand-holding to combat without transition because she is always drawn to whatever is most stimulating in the moment.

Wing Influence (7w8):

The w8 wing gives Opal her physical assertiveness. She does not just want to experience things — she wants to dominate them. "I'm going to just chop the snake" is pure 8-wing energy: confrontational, direct, physical. Her Goliath strength is an extension of this — she meets the world with force, not finesse.

Integration (Growth) Direction — Type 5:

Opal shows fleeting Type 5 moments when she pauses to observe. Her animal handling check (17!) shows genuine perceptive capability when she focuses. Her question "Does the snake speak common?" reveals a brief analytical impulse.

Disintegration (Stress) Direction — Type 1:

Under stress, Opal becomes rigid and frustrated. When her rolls fail repeatedly (the mirror push, the intimidation check), she puts dice "in timeout" and shows visible irritation with herself. The jovial energy compresses into self-criticism.

Enneagram Summary:

Opal is a 7w8 who charges through life with arms open, ready to embrace or fight whatever comes next. Her growth edge is learning to sit with discomfort rather than immediately reframing or attacking it. The tension between her desire to experience everything and the session's time pressure creates her central arc — she wants to loot, explore, and befriend every creature, but the clock is ticking.

BIG FIVE (OCEAN) PERSONALITY SCORES

Trait Score (1-10) Label Evidence
Openness to Experience 7 High Opal is curious and enthusiastic about every new thing. She wants to smell dinosaurs, touch pressure plates, get tattoos, and befriend snakes. She is not intellectually open (she does not engage with abstractions), but she is experientially open — she wants to do everything.
Conscientiousness 2 Very Low Opal does not plan, organize, or prepare. She acts on impulse: "I go get it. I want it." She forgets mechanics, loses track of her own abilities, and puts dice in timeout rather than adjusting her strategy. She is reactive, not proactive.
Extraversion 10 Very High Opal is the most externally energized character in the party. She talks constantly, reacts to everything, reaches out to others when vulnerable, and fills silence with commentary. Her energy is always directed outward.
Agreeableness 8 High Opal is warm, trusting, and cooperative. She holds Lavender's hand when blind, she thanks Moor for healing, she is "delighted by everyone." She does not compete with allies or hoard resources. She offers her Pearl of Power to someone who can use it better.
Neuroticism 4 Low-Average Opal is emotionally reactive but recovers quickly. She expresses frustration openly ("that fucking sucks") but does not dwell on it. Going blind is a momentary crisis, not a spiral. She is resilient through reframing and social connection.

OCEAN Profile Visualization:

```

Openness: [███████───] 7/10

Conscientiousness: [██────────] 2/10

Extraversion: [██████████] 10/10

Agreeableness: [████████──] 8/10

Neuroticism: [████──────] 4/10

```

Profile Interpretation:

High E, high A, very low C — this is the profile of someone who is enormously fun to be around but should not be put in charge of logistics. Opal brings warmth, energy, and willingness to every situation but contributes chaos as much as capability. The high openness means she will try anything; the low conscientiousness means she will not follow up. This combination makes her an ideal party member (enthusiastic, generous, brave) and a terrible project manager.

BEHAVIORAL PATTERNS

Decision-Making Style

Instantaneous and impulse-driven. Opal decides by feeling, not analysis. "I go get it. I want it." "I'm going to just chop the snake." Her decisions are fast, physical, and rarely reconsidered.

Evidence: When she sees the obsidian rock under the mechanism, she does not ask about consequences — "I go get it. I want it." The DM has to physically stop her to explain it is attached to something.

Conflict Response

Direct physical engagement. Opal's response to every threat is to charge. She rages, she chops, she pushes. She does not retreat, negotiate, or analyze. Her blind snake kill (natural crit with the greataxe) is the purest expression of this.

Evidence: "What if I just chop the snake?" — Opal's conflict style in five words.

Leadership Style

Not a leader but an enthusiastic follower who volunteers for everything. Opal does not direct others but throws herself into whatever the group decides. She amplifies energy rather than directing it.

Evidence: "I'm happy to be up front." "I can do that." "I'll take the damage and run through it if you want."

Stress Indicators

Opal's stress manifests as self-deprecating humor and dice frustration. When rolls fail, she becomes visibly irritated — putting dice "in timeout," saying "I'm having the worst day." Her verbal output increases under stress rather than decreasing.

Evidence: After the natural 1 on the mirror push, leading to blindness: "Cool! You're behind there, yeah. That one's in timeout."

Attachment Style (Interpersonal)

Secure. Opal trusts easily, reaches out for connection when vulnerable, and does not fear dependence or rejection. She holds Lavender's hand without embarrassment and openly asks for help when blind.

Evidence: "Someone's gotta tell me where to go and what to do. I hold your hand. Okay. I hold Lavender's hand. And then I'm like, this is very nice, thank you."

FICTIONAL CHARACTER COMPARISON

"Opal is the Kronk of the party."

Comparison: Kronk from The Emperor's New Groove

Why this comparison works:

Both are physically enormous, enormously enthusiastic, and operate at a lower intellectual register than their companions without it diminishing their value. Kronk's earnest, good-hearted energy — his desire to please, his delight in simple pleasures, his surprising competence when the moment calls for it — mirrors Opal perfectly. Both are the muscle who everyone underestimates until they deliver in the clutch. Opal's blind crit-kill on the snake is Kronk suddenly speaking squirrel: unexpected excellence from an unexpected source.

Where the comparison breaks down:

Kronk has a people-pleasing streak driven by insecurity (his father's disapproval) that Opal lacks entirely. Opal's self-confidence is genuine and uncomplicated. She does not need validation — she is simply happy to be here.

Aramil — Personality Analysis

Half-Elf Warlock (Archaeologist) | Played by Kyle 2

MYERS-BRIGGS TYPE INDICATOR: ENTP

"The Debater" — Clever, strategic, morally flexible, and always angling

Dimension Breakdown:

Extraversion (E) vs. Introversion (I): E

Assessment: Moderate E

Evidence:

  • Aramil talks frequently and engages with multiple party members simultaneously. He offers unsolicited advice, interjects into others' plans, and volunteers opinions readily: "I think we need to kill the snake." He processes out loud.
  • He is socially engaged but selectively — he speaks when he has something clever or strategic to contribute, not for social warmth. His extroversion is instrumental rather than purely social.

Key moment: His beast-speech conversation with the Gorallon — Aramil does not just communicate information, he performs: "Hey, big guy. You just saw a lover boy get blasted by this, right? You want that to happen to you? Think about your wife and kids."

Sensing (S) vs. Intuition (N): N

Assessment: Clear N

Evidence:

  • Aramil is the party's pattern-seeker and idea-generator. He studies murals for meaning, connects the snake idol to the sun disk, understands the implication of removing it ("you may actually throw the land into eternal darkness"), and responds "I'm cool with that" — because he sees possibilities, not just present consequences.
  • He generates creative solutions constantly: "What if the T-Rex stands up, would someone standing on its head be tall enough to reach the idol?" "Can I inception the T-Rex?" "What if I Phantasmal Force a pack of raptors?"

Key moment: His interpretation of the murals — seeing the connection between the serpent, the sun disk, and the potential for eternal darkness, then calculating whether the consequences are worth the reward.

Thinking (T) vs. Feeling (F): T

Assessment: Clear T

Evidence:

  • Aramil's decisions are driven by self-interest and logical calculation, not empathy or group harmony. He knows that stealing the golden eye will plunge the world into darkness and says: "If it gets me reinstated at the GYL Community College, then we're okay." His moral calculus is purely transactional.
  • When asked about the snake, his approach is strategic manipulation, not emotional connection: "Think about your wife and kids" is not empathy — it is a calculated persuasion tactic.
  • His response to Lavender's offer to trade the Rod of the Pactkeeper: "Get your fucking rope" — direct, unsentimentalized, strategic.

Key moment: "You know, if it gets me reinstated at the GYL Community College, then we're okay." — Self-interest as the explicit basis for a world-altering decision.

Judging (J) vs. Perceiving (P): P

Assessment: Moderate P

Evidence:

  • Aramil keeps options open and adapts. He does not commit to plans prematurely — he gathers information (studying murals), explores alternatives (speaking with animals, Phantasmal Force, locate object), and adjusts as new data arrives.
  • He is comfortable with ambiguity and moral gray areas. He does not need closure or certainty to act — he is fine with "I don't entirely know what this will do, but I'll do it anyway."
  • However, he shows some J tendencies in his intellectual confidence. He lectures about exothermic biology with professorial authority: "They're exothermic, which means they rely on external sources of heat."

Key moment: When the party debates how to get the idol, Aramil generates multiple possible approaches rather than committing to one: Dimension Door, Mage Hand, riding the T-Rex, climbing.

MBTI Summary:

Aramil's ENTP manifests as a morally flexible intellectual who uses cleverness as his primary tool. He is the party's idea machine — generating options, seeing patterns, and making connections others miss. But his ENTP shadow is visible too: he can be selfish, manipulative, and willing to sacrifice others' welfare for his own ambitions. The "adjunct professor emeritus" backstory is the perfect ENTP wound — intellectual brilliance unrecognized by institutions, driving him toward increasingly desperate bids for validation.

ENNEAGRAM: Type 3 — "The Achiever"

Wing: 3w4

Core Motivation: To be recognized as exceptional — specifically by his father and by academic institutions. Aramil does not just want the idol; he wants what the idol represents: proof of his worth. "Maybe my dad finally noticed me."

Core Fear: Being seen as a failure, a fraud, or irrelevant. His "adjunct professor emeritus" title is a thin veneer over being fired, and he knows it: when Lavender says "So you're fired," his response ("I am adjunct professor emeritus. Thank you.") is defensive deflection.

Evidence for Type 3:

  • Aramil's self-presentation is carefully curated to project competence: the fedora, the title, the academic language. But the gap between image and reality is part of his character — he thinks he looks like Indiana Jones but actually looks "dingy and dirty."
  • He steals the golden eye at the end — secretly, with an 18 on sleight of hand — not for the party, not for Fizzleblast, but for himself. This is a Type 3 move: acquiring a symbol of achievement regardless of the consequences to others.
  • His knowledge-dropping about exothermic biology is performative competence — he is showing the group (and himself) that he is the smart one.

Wing Influence (3w4):

The w4 adds a melancholic, individualistic streak. Aramil does not just want success — he wants to be uniquely brilliant. His archaeological obsession, his purple crystal necklace, his grungy aesthetic — these are w4 markers of someone who wants to stand out, not just succeed.

Integration (Growth) Direction — Type 6:

In moments of security, Aramil shows Type 6 loyalty and teamwork. He uses Speak with Animals to protect the group, he provides Guidance to allies, and his Phantasmal Force during the escape is used to save everyone, not just himself. When the pressure is highest, his selfishness recedes.

Disintegration (Stress) Direction — Type 9:

Under stress, Aramil disengages. When Opal is struggling with the mirror, he says "I'm appreciating art" — literally retreating into passive observation rather than helping with the physical task. When grappled by the snake and dropped to -10 HP, he goes completely passive.

Enneagram Summary:

Aramil is a 3w4 driven by the desperate need to prove himself to a father who may never acknowledge him. His academic credentials are a shield for deep insecurity, and his willingness to plunge Faerun into darkness for personal gain reveals how far a wounded 3 will go for validation. His growth direction — toward genuine loyalty and teamwork — is visible in his best moments, making him a character with real redemptive potential.

BIG FIVE (OCEAN) PERSONALITY SCORES

Trait Score (1-10) Label Evidence
Openness to Experience 9 Very High Aramil is the most intellectually curious character. He studies murals, generates creative solutions, reads the mythology of the temple, and approaches problems from multiple theoretical angles. He has the highest "what if" count in the party.
Conscientiousness 4 Low-Average Aramil is capable of focus when interested (mural study, plotting) but unreliable when the task is mundane ("I'm appreciating art" while others do physical labor). He is a classic high-openness/low-conscientiousness intellectual — brilliant but undisciplined.
Extraversion 6 Average-High Aramil engages readily in conversation and volunteers ideas but is not the life of the party. He is more of a contributor than a center-of-attention. His extroversion is intellectual rather than social.
Agreeableness 3 Low Aramil is self-interested and willing to betray the group's trust for personal gain (the golden eye theft). He manipulates rather than empathizes (the Gorallon persuasion). He trades grudgingly ("Get your fucking rope"). He is not cruel, but he is not warm.
Neuroticism 5 Average Aramil shows anxiety about his status ("Maybe my dad finally noticed me") and defensive reactions to challenges to his identity ("I am adjunct professor emeritus"). But he does not spiral — he channels anxiety into action (stealing the eye, pushing forward).

OCEAN Profile Visualization:

```

Openness: [█████████─] 9/10

Conscientiousness: [████──────] 4/10

Extraversion: [██████────] 6/10

Agreeableness: [███───────] 3/10

Neuroticism: [█████─────] 5/10

```

Profile Interpretation:

Very high O, low A, average N — this is the profile of a brilliant, self-serving opportunist. Aramil sees patterns and possibilities everywhere but filters them through "how does this benefit me?" The average neuroticism means he is not paralyzed by his insecurity, but it fuels his ambition. The low agreeableness combined with high openness produces someone who is fascinating to talk to but dangerous to trust.

BEHAVIORAL PATTERNS

Decision-Making Style

Strategic and self-interested. Aramil gathers information first (murals, animal communication, perception), then acts decisively when the opportunity aligns with his interests. He is patient when planning and impulsive when executing.

Evidence: He waited until the entire session was nearly over, then executed a perfect sleight of hand steal of the golden eye — the decision was premeditated the moment he read the murals.

Conflict Response

Manipulation and cleverness over direct confrontation. Aramil does not fight if he can persuade, trick, or redirect. His beast-speech negotiation with the Gorallon is a perfect example: rather than fighting, he intimidates through information asymmetry.

Evidence: "Hey, big guy. Think about your wife and kids." — Manipulative threat packaged as friendly advice.

Leadership Style

Intellectual advisor. Aramil does not lead from the front but positions himself as the person with the answers. He suggests strategies, identifies patterns, and delegates physical labor to others. He is an armchair general.

Evidence: "Calmly suggest to the more muscle inclined of us to try to point the light at the crystal." He identifies the solution but has someone else execute it.

Stress Indicators

Under stress, Aramil becomes either hyperverbal (generating rapid-fire ideas) or passive ("I'm appreciating art"). His stress response depends on whether the threat is to his body (passive withdrawal) or his intellect (verbal acceleration).

Evidence: When constricted by the snake (-10 HP), complete passivity. When debating how to get the idol, rapid idea generation: "What if the T-Rex stands up? Can I inception it? Mage Hand? Dimension Door?"

Attachment Style (Interpersonal)

Dismissive-avoidant. Aramil does not form visible emotional bonds. His relationships are transactional — he helps others when it serves the mission (his mission, specifically). He deflects vulnerability with humor: "Maybe my dad finally noticed me" is delivered as a joke, not a confession.

Evidence: When offered the chance to make friends with the rival party, his response is immediate: "Nope." No consideration, no curiosity about other people as people.

FICTIONAL CHARACTER COMPARISON

"Aramil is the Belloq of the party — if Belloq were also Indiana Jones."

Comparison: A hybrid of Indiana Jones and Belloq from Raiders of the Lost Ark

Why this comparison works:

Aramil is explicitly modeled on Indiana Jones — the fedora, the archaeology, the adventuring academic. But the key insight is that he has become his own villain. Like Belloq, he is willing to take the sacred artifact regardless of consequences, motivated by personal glory rather than preservation. The "adjunct professor emeritus" title mirrors Indy's academic credentials, but the golden eye theft — knowingly plunging the world into darkness for a resume line — is pure Belloq. He is both the hero and the rival, the researcher and the looter, the academic and the fraud.

Where the comparison breaks down:

Belloq is cold and deliberate; Aramil has genuine flashes of warmth and loyalty (using Speak with Animals to save the group, providing Guidance, using Phantasmal Force during the escape). He is not a true villain — he is a good person making selfish choices under pressure, which is more interesting and more human.

Lavender — Personality Analysis

Tiefling Warlock (Genie/Chain Pact) | Played by Kelsey

MYERS-BRIGGS TYPE INDICATOR: ENFJ

"The Protagonist" — Socially adept, strategic, caring, and action-oriented

Dimension Breakdown:

Extraversion (E) vs. Introversion (I): E

Assessment: Moderate E

Evidence:

  • Lavender engages actively with every party member, initiating conversations, offering solutions, and coordinating group action. She is the social glue of the party — she gives Opal a bandana when she is blind, asks about other characters' backstories, and keeps dialogue flowing: "How are you? Hi, sorry."
  • She thinks out loud, processing options verbally: "Do you think I should break that necklace?" "Should we make friends with the other friends?" She generates energy through group interaction.

Key moment: "If the men find out we can shapeshift, they're going to tell the church." — Lavender deploys humor socially, connecting with others through wit rather than withdrawing into it.

Sensing (S) vs. Intuition (N): N

Assessment: Moderate N

Evidence:

  • Lavender sees the bigger picture and anticipates consequences. She is the first to worry about the rival party: "Think about that other team that's coming that's going to kill us." She anticipates the Dimension Door being used at the end before it happens: "I can picture him using it to get out at the very end."
  • She connects patterns across situations — recognizing the pressure plate system, the mirror mechanics, and the genie's wind signal without needing explicit instruction.
  • She also shows some S groundedness in her practical focus on immediate utility: "I feel like having +2 to my armor class is more [important]."

Key moment: "It's concerning that we get so many of these good things. Classic, playing the video game, you're like, cool, I just got 10 new lives. Couldn't mean anything, bad's coming." — Pattern recognition across meta-narrative.

Thinking (T) vs. Feeling (F): F

Assessment: Moderate F

Evidence:

  • Lavender's interpersonal warmth is consistent and genuine. She gives Opal a bandana and holds her hand when she is blind. She worries about the rival team's feelings: "See, they're nervous and they're scared, but they're probably friends." She brings emotional awareness to tactical situations.
  • However, her F is not naive — she is strategically empathetic. She can deploy warmth when useful (carrying Fizzleblast's hat, booping Opal's head) and turn it off when needed (agreeing with "we're not here to make friends" once overruled).
  • She advocates for group welfare over individual gain: "Don't waste on me, though" when healing is being distributed.

Key moment: "I come over and I give you another very pretty bandana to put over your eyes. And I boop you on the head." — Spontaneous caregiving during a crisis.

Judging (J) vs. Perceiving (P): J

Assessment: Moderate J

Evidence:

  • Lavender pushes for decisions and forward momentum. She is frequently the one saying "Let's not think about it too long," "Later. Later. Later," "We should just keep moving." She wants the group to commit and act rather than deliberating endlessly.
  • She plans ahead: dropping her crossbow for inventory space early, anticipating the escape route, worrying about time management. She is the party's project manager.
  • She closes loops: "Can I tell her what I'm doing? We're looting." — She keeps people informed and aligned.

Key moment: "Let's not think about it too long." — Lavender's J-drive to close debates and move forward.

MBTI Summary:

Lavender's ENFJ is the party's social center and strategic coordinator. She combines genuine interpersonal warmth with tactical acumen — she cares about people AND about efficiency, and she does not see these as contradictory. Her warlock abilities (imp familiar, genie vessel, Gust of Wind, Hellish Rebuke) are extensions of her personality: versatile tools used adaptively to protect the group. She is the session MVP not because she did the most damage, but because she made everyone else more effective.

ENNEAGRAM: Type 2 — "The Helper"

Wing: 2w3

Core Motivation: To be needed and valued by the group — not through subordination, but through indispensable contribution. Lavender does not just want to help; she wants to be the person without whom things fall apart.

Core Fear: Being useless or excluded. When she cannot contribute physically ("I'm not first"), she immediately pivots to another way to contribute: sending Tika, using Gust of Wind, researching the genie's message.

Evidence for Type 2:

  • Lavender's first instinct in every new situation is "how can I help?" She gives Opal her shield, her bandana, and her hand. She sends Tika to loot for the group. She casts Gust of Wind to save the party from poison gas. She Hellish Rebukes the baboon that attacks her — even her combat is protective.
  • She searches for loot on behalf of others: "I'll give one to Kyle. I'll give the other one to Kyle." She does not hoard; she distributes.
