Kelsey / Lavender
For keeping a cool head while her lungs were filled with poison, proving that the best solution to a lethal problem is often just a very strong breeze.
Runner-Up: Kyle 2 / Aramil
🏆
Most Creative Problem Solving
Kelsey / Lavender
For realizing that the treasure wasn't just loot—it was a highly effective and very expensive paperweight for murder traps.
Runner-Up: Shannon / Opal
🏆
Most Dramatic Moment
Kyle 2 / Aramil
For a brush with death so intimate it required a literal miracle from the Cleric to bring him back from the brink of the afterlife.
Runner-Up: Josh / Bob
🏆
Most In-Character Moment
Kyle 2 / Aramil
For maintaining the bitter, academic resentment of an underpaid adjunct even while being pursued by prehistoric monsters.
Runner-Up: Shannon / Opal
🏆
Most Likely to Betray the Party
Kyle 2 / Aramil
For successfully completing the mission while simultaneously dooming the world to a sunless apocalypse. We hope that tenure was worth it.
Runner-Up: Josh / Bob
🏆
Funniest Moment
The Entire Table
For collectively derailing the high-fantasy tension within the first ten minutes to establish the most questionable economic system in D&D history.
Runner-Up: Brandon (DM)
🏆
The "Peak D&D" Award
The Entire Table
For the moment where the line between game and reality blurred, and five friends genuinely panicked together to save their imaginary lives.
Runner-Up: N/A
🏆
Most Damage in a Single Turn
Shannon / Opal
For proving that a Goliath’s fury is the most efficient way to turn a 40-foot apex predator into several 5-foot appetizers.
Runner-Up: Kelsey / Lavender
🏆
Unluckiest Roll
Shannon / Opal
For failing so spectacularly that the universe decided to turn off the lights for sixty minutes. A truly radiant disaster.
Runner-Up: Kyle 1 / Moor
🏆
Most Chaotic Action
Shannon / Opal
For refusing to let a minor inconvenience like 'total blindness' stop her from wrestling with furniture in the middle of a dungeon.
Runner-Up: Josh / Bob
🏆
"I Can't Believe That Worked" Award
Kelsey / Lavender & Kyle 1 / Moor
For turning a deadly gauntlet into a high-stakes rehearsal of a middle-school field day event—and surviving.
Runner-Up: Josh / Bob
🏆
Table Energy Award
Shannon (Player)
For being the spark plug of the session, turning every failed roll into a comedy routine and every success into a victory parade.
Runner-Up: Kelsey (Player)
Biggest Moment

Moments

The Biggest Single Moment — Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip

The Moment

What: Aramil secretly pried the golden eye from the Snake Idol as the party boarded the canoes to leave, plunging the entire continent of Faerun into eternal darkness.

Who: Aramil (Half-Elf Warlock), played by Kyle 2

When: The very end of the session — after the party had survived the temple, the T-Rex, the rival adventurers, and the skill challenge escape. The mission was over. They were safe.

Trigger: A deliberate, secret player decision. No dice forced this. No DM reveal prompted it. Aramil saw the idol, saw the gem, and chose to take it — alone, unobserved, without consulting the party.

Impact Scoring

Each candidate moment is scored across five axes, each rated 1-10.

Scoring Rubric

Axis Definition What a 10 Looks Like What a 1 Looks Like
Narrative Consequence How much does this moment change the story going forward? Campaign trajectory permanently altered. New quests, enemies, or alliances created. The world is different because of this moment. No lasting story impact. Forgotten by next scene.
Irreversibility Can this be undone? Absolutely not. A character died, a bridge was burned, a secret was revealed, an artifact was destroyed. No take-backs. Easily reversible. A short rest, a retcon, or a simple conversation fixes it.
Cascade Effect How many downstream consequences does this moment trigger? Multiple immediate consequences that each spawn their own consequences. A domino chain of repercussions. Self-contained. The moment happened and then the session moved on.
Emotional Impact How strongly did this moment affect the people at the table? Players gasped, shouted, went silent, or otherwise had a visible emotional response. The moment was FELT. Nobody reacted beyond "okay, cool."
Improbability How unlikely was this moment? Astronomically unlikely. A nat 20 at the perfect time, a decision nobody saw coming, a coincidence that defies belief. Expected and predictable. This was always going to happen.

The Winning Moment's Scores

Axis Score (1-10) Justification
Narrative Consequence 10 The sun will not rise again in Faerun. This is not a local effect or a temporary debuff — it is a continental, cosmological catastrophe. The DM explicitly confirmed that this act sets up the "Tube of Annihilation" campaign, meaning an entire future campaign arc exists because of this single theft. Every living creature on the continent is affected.
Irreversibility 9 The eye is out. The sun is gone. Fizzleblast was asleep and nobody saw it happen. The only reason this is not a 10 is that a future campaign could, theoretically, reverse the curse — but within the scope of this session and this party's ability, it is absolutely permanent. There is no spell, no short rest, no conversation that undoes "the sun stopped rising."
Cascade Effect 10 The immediate cascade is staggering: eternal darkness means the undead rise permanently, the mirror defense system of the temple is permanently offline, cold-blooded creatures like the T-Rex lose their heat source, all of Chult's ecology collapses, Vecna's plan advances, and the party has unknowingly become the instrument of apocalypse. The DM named Vecna as the architect behind this outcome. Every thread of the one-shot's worldbuilding — the mirrors, the thermal arrays, the light-dependent defenses, the nocturnal undead — inverts.
Emotional Impact 9 The table erupted. "Not cool, man." "You monster." "Shit." The DM's reveal landed like a bomb — the casual sleight of hand check followed by "And you have plunged the entire continent into eternal darkness" is one of the sharpest tonal pivots in the session. The laughter and disbelief were immediate and sustained. The only reason this is not a 10 is that the moment came after the session's tension had already resolved; the party was safe, so the emotional register was more shock-and-delight than dread.
Improbability 7 Aramil's character was established from the start as an opportunistic, disgraced academic desperate for his father's approval. The Gloves of Thievery gave him a +6 to Sleight of Hand. The character was built to steal things. But nobody at the table — not even the DM, based on the pacing — expected a player to casually pocket a world-ending artifact as a post-credits stinger. The 18 on the Sleight of Hand check sealed it, but it was the audacity of the decision that surprised everyone, not the mechanical outcome.
TOTAL 45/50

The Full Writeup

Paragraph 1: The Setup

They had done it. Against odds that the DM himself admitted he thought were insurmountable, Charisma Company had cleared the Temple of the Man and Crocodile, survived a T-Rex stirring awake beneath them, weathered a fireball from a rival party, and sprinted through a real-time skill challenge with four minutes and twenty seconds on the clock. They had collapsed into Fizzleblast's safety bubble as the undead clawed out of the Chultan soil behind them, and the gnome wizard snored through the entire apocalypse outside his force field. The tension was gone. The dice were down. The session was in its epilogue — players comparing loot, laughing about close calls, marveling that they were the first party in the DM's run of this one-shot to get everyone out alive. It was, in every way, the exhale after the held breath.

Paragraph 2: The Moment Itself

Aramil's player asked about the Eye of the Serpent — a golden gem set into the Snake Idol that Bob had already stashed in his pack. The DM confirmed the gem was accessible. "I rolled an 18 on the sleight of hand to take it," Aramil's player said, almost casually. "As we're getting in the boat, I'm going to be like..." The DM processed it for a beat. "As you pull it out, nobody sees you taking it. The other eye is still intact. Nobody sees it because Blast is asleep. He doesn't know that you took it." A pause. Then: "And you have plunged the entire continent into eternal darkness." The table went silent for exactly one second before the reactions hit. The DM continued, his voice carrying the weight of someone who had been waiting for this moment the entire session: "The sun will not rise again in Faerun."

Paragraph 3: The Immediate Aftermath

"Not cool, man." "Shit." "You monster." The reactions came fast, overlapping, half-laughing and half-horrified. The DM leaned into it — Vecna was invoked, the Tube of Annihilation campaign was teased, and the table collectively realized that the real final boss of the session was not the T-Rex, not the rival party, not the constrictor snake, but a single player character's greed. Aramil's player, for his part, leaned into it with the energy of someone who knew exactly what he was doing. "Seems like we need to play that next," he said. The session's triumphant ending — the first full-party survival in the DM's history of running this one-shot — was instantly and permanently recontextualized. They survived the dungeon, but the dungeon's real trap was the golden eye, and Aramil walked right into it with his Gloves of Thievery and an 18 on the check.

Paragraph 4: The Ripple Effects

The consequences are literally planetary. The heliographic thermal arrays that protected the temple — the entire defense mechanism the party spent the session navigating — are now permanently offline. The T-Rex, the Gorallons, the crocodiles, the undead that only rose at night: the ecology of Chult and, by extension, all of Faerun pivots on the day-night cycle, and that cycle is now broken. The undead no longer wait for sunset. The mirror-based traps no longer function. Every faction, every creature, every NPC the party encountered exists in a fundamentally different world now. And none of the other party members know. Bob is carrying the idol. Moor healed everyone. Lavender found the Zephyr Reliquary. Opal killed the snake blind. They all contributed to the mission's success. But Aramil, secretly and alone, undid it all with a single Sleight of Hand check while everyone else was celebrating.

Paragraph 5: The Deeper Significance

There is something almost poetically inevitable about this moment. Aramil was introduced as a washed-up Indiana Jones — a disgraced adjunct professor emeritus from the Royal Community College, desperate for his father's approval, grungy and paranoid and opportunistic. The Gloves of Thievery that bound to him at the start of the session were not just a mechanical advantage; they were a character statement. The DM gave this man the tools to steal, and then placed a world-ending gem within arm's reach. Aramil did not steal the eye because he knew what it would do. He stole it because that is who he is — a man so hungry for validation that he would pocket a cursed artifact and call it a career achievement. "I will bring it back to the community college," he said. "I will put it in my office." The sun is gone, the dead walk, and Aramil is planning his display case. It is the most perfectly in-character act of the entire session, and it is the one that ends the world.

"What Would Have Happened Otherwise"

The Alternate Timeline

If Aramil had not pried the golden eye from the Snake Idol, the session would have ended as the DM clearly designed it to end in the best-case scenario: a clean, triumphant extraction. The party returns to Fizzleblast with the idol intact, collects their payment, and sails home with their loot and their stories. The sun rises the next morning as it always has. The mirror defense system of the temple continues to function, keeping the jungle's predators at bay. The undead remain nocturnal. Chult remains dangerous but navigable. The "Tube of Annihilation" campaign — whatever form it takes — either does not exist or is triggered by some other catalyst entirely.

What the Party Doesn't Realize They Avoided (or Caused)

The party does not know about the eye theft. This is critical. Bob has the idol. Opal never got her obsidian rock. Lavender is attuned to the Zephyr Reliquary. Moor has the Scales of Justice. Everyone is accounted for — except for the gem that was never supposed to leave the idol's socket. When the sun fails to rise, the party will have no immediate reason to connect it to their mission. Only Aramil knows, and he has every reason to stay silent. The DM's invocation of Vecna suggests that this was not just a magical security system but a cosmological anchor — the golden eyes of the serpent idol were literally holding the sun in place. Aramil did not just steal a gem. He removed a linchpin from the architecture of the world. And the party, if they ever figure it out, will have to reckon with the fact that one of their own did this — not under mind control, not under duress, but because he wanted something shiny for his office.

The Butterfly Effect

Without the eternal darkness, Faerun continues on its existing trajectory. The rival party is dead regardless — devoured by the undead outside Fizzleblast's bubble. The T-Rex still lost its heat lamp and would eventually need to relocate. But these are local, manageable consequences. With the eternal darkness, the entire continent restructures. Cities without magical light sources become indefensible. Agricultural systems collapse. Trade routes through wilderness become suicide runs. Nocturnal predators and undead gain permanent advantage. Fizzleblast's expedition, which he considered a triumph of his "Charisma Company" brand, becomes the inciting incident of a continental catastrophe. And Aramil — adjunct professor emeritus, wielder of the Gloves of Thievery, stealer of the Eye of the Serpent — walks back into civilization with a cursed gem in his pocket and no idea that he has just become the most consequential person on the continent.

The Transcript Excerpt

```

Lavender,we encounter this? Oh, yeah. I want to know more about this, too. It's in the eye

DM,of the serpent. Of the idol. Okay. If you wanted to go get it, you would see it. Go ahead. All right.

Aramil,I rolled an 18 on the sleight of hand to take it. As we're getting in the boat,

DM,I'm going to be like... As you pull it out, nobody sees you taking it. The other eye is still intact. Nobody sees it because Blast is asleep. He doesn't know that you

DM,took it.

DM,And you have plunged the entire continent into eternal darkness.

Opal,Not cool, man. The

DM,sun will not rise again in Faerun. Shit.

Opal,My obsidian state. Vecna

DM,is laughing as this was his plan. You have set yourself up for the Tube of Annihilation

Opal,campaign.

Bob,You monster.

Aramil,Seems like we need to play that next.

```

Context for the Excerpt: The party had just completed the entire dungeon, survived the skill challenge escape, and made it safely into Fizzleblast's protective bubble on the beach. The session was effectively over — players were discussing loot, comparing notes, and basking in the fact that they were the first group to ever get the full party out alive. Lavender's player asked about the Eye of the Serpent, and Aramil's player seized the opening.

Honorable Mentions

Honorable Mention #1: Opal Kills the Giant Constrictor Snake While Blind

Axis Score
Narrative Consequence 6
Irreversibility 7
Cascade Effect 6
Emotional Impact 8
Improbability 8
TOTAL 35/50

What Happened: After rolling a Natural 1 on a mirror puzzle and taking 14 radiant damage directly to her eyes, Opal spent most of the mid-session completely blind. When the Giant Constrictor Snake emerged and began squeezing Aramil to -10 HP, Opal — still unable to see — rolled a critical hit with her Great Axe for 30 damage, chopping through the coiled layers of the snake like a cake and killing it outright. She freed the unconscious Aramil and ended the fight in a single swing she could not see.

Why It Didn't Win: As thrilling and improbable as the blind crit was, its consequences were contained within the combat encounter. The snake dies, Aramil gets healed, the party moves on. It did not change the trajectory of the story or the world — it resolved a crisis rather than creating one.

Honorable Mention #2: Moor Melts the Gorallon with the Thermal Mirror

Axis Score
Narrative Consequence 5
Irreversibility 7
Cascade Effect 5
Emotional Impact 7
Improbability 7
TOTAL 31/50

What Happened: When two Gorallons (four-armed jungle apes) charged the party at the temple entrance, Moor had a flash of tactical brilliance: instead of fighting them conventionally, he asked to turn one of the heliographic thermal mirrors and aim it directly at the creature. He rolled an 18 on the Strength check to rotate the massive mirror, and the focused beam of concentrated sunlight burned a hole clean through the Gorallon's torso, melting it from the inside out. The DM described the smell as "cooked steak plus burnt hair." This was the first creative use of the temple's environment, and it set the template the party would use for the rest of the session.

Why It Didn't Win: The Gorallon fight was a gatekeeping encounter at the temple entrance. Moor's ingenuity was impressive and set a tone of environmental problem-solving, but the Gorallons were obstacles, not story-changers. The fight's outcome did not alter the session's trajectory — the party was always going to enter the temple.

Honorable Mention #3: The Sun Goes Down and the Rival Party Launches a Fireball

Axis Score
Narrative Consequence 7
Irreversibility 8
Cascade Effect 8
Emotional Impact 8
Improbability 5
TOTAL 36/50

What Happened: Just as Bob successfully snatched the Golden Snake Idol from the archway above the sleeping T-Rex — nailing stealth checks with a Dirty 20 and a 21 — the rival adventuring party launched a Fireball from above. Bob took 9 damage (halved by his Dex save of 24), but the real consequence was that the Fireball hit the T-Rex at full force, waking it in a rage while simultaneously destroying its nest and the remaining mirror infrastructure. This triggered the real-time skill challenge: four minutes and twenty seconds to reach Fizzleblast's bubble, with a death on the line at three failures. The session's entire climax — the frantic escape, the pressure plate blades, the rope-pulling, the dimension-dooring mages — all cascaded from this single ambush.

Why It Didn't Win: The Fireball was an external event — a DM-driven trigger designed to initiate the climactic sequence. It was dramatic and consequential within the session, but it was a narrative device rather than a player-driven turning point. The rival party's attack was always going to happen in some form; the party had been warned about them from the very beginning.

Session Impact Summary

Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip was a one-shot designed as a heist: get in, get the idol, get out alive. The party accomplished all of that. They solved puzzles, killed a snake, dodged a T-Rex, outran the undead, and made it to safety with four and a half minutes to spare — the first full-party survival in the DM's history of running this adventure. And then, in the final moments, a single player reached into his pocket and ended the world. Aramil's theft of the Eye of the Serpent is the kind of moment that redefines everything that came before it. Every clever mirror trick, every clutch healing spell, every desperate skill check — all of it becomes prologue to a single Sleight of Hand roll that the rest of the party did not even notice. If someone asks "what happened in Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip?" the answer is not the T-Rex, not the blind crit, not the fireball. The answer is: a washed-up professor stole a gem, and the sun went out. This is not a moment that will be referenced in future sessions. It is the moment that creates future sessions — the Tube of Annihilation campaign exists because Aramil could not leave well enough alone.

Post-Session Interviews

Interviews

Here are the post-session character interviews for Charisma Company, conducted by Elswyth Pattle, the field chronicler for Mercenaries, Murders, and Mothers-in-Law Monthly.

Interview: Moor

Dragonborn Cleric (Life Domain) | Interviewed immediately upon returning to camp

Setting

[Moor is found sitting heavily on a log near the beached canoes. He has removed his helmet and is methodically cleaning a mixture of gorilla gore and snake slime off his mace with a rag. He looks like a parent who has just survived a toddlers' birthday party at a trampoline park. He does not look up as Elswyth approaches.]

Q&A

Elswyth Pattle: Master Moor? Thank you for sitting with me. That was... quite the extraction. How are you feeling?

[Moor stops scrubbing. He stares at a dent in his shield. He exhales a puff of smoke from his nostrils.]

Moor: Tired. Sticky.

[He resumes scrubbing, harder.]

Elswyth Pattle: You seemed to take on the role of protector for the group. Is that a calling of Tyr, or just your nature?

[He pauses, looking over his shoulder at where Opal is loudly recounting a story to a tree.]

Moor: Necessity. Someone has to stand in front. Someone has to carry the shield. If I don't, they die. They are squishy. I am scales. It is simple math.

Elswyth Pattle: Let’s talk about the Temple. The "Man and Crocodile" puzzle. You and Lavender had to coordinate perfectly to cross those pressure plates. What was going through your mind during that?

[Moor shifts. He looks uncomfortable. He taps the rag against his knee.]

Moor: She is... light. Very light. I was worried the plate would not click. Then I was worried she would step too fast. It was a trust exercise. I do not like trust exercises. I prefer hammers. Hammers are predictable. Tieflings with genies are not.

Elswyth Pattle: You had a significant moment with the undead—the skeletons in the gas room. You turned almost all of them to dust instantly.

[A rare, toothy grin flickers across his face, then vanishes.]

Moor: Tyr was watching. The scales must be balanced. Undead are heavy on the wrong side. I simply... removed the weight. It was efficient. I like efficiency.

Elswyth Pattle: Speaking of efficiency, you healed your companions repeatedly. Aramil went down, Opal was blind... is it frustrating?

[He sighs, a low, rumbling growl deep in his chest.]

Moor: It is the job. Aramil talks. I heal. Opal smashes. I heal. Bob... Bob is Bob. I heal. If I stop, the talking and smashing stops. Then it is just quiet. I like quiet. But I do not like dead friends. So, I heal.

Elswyth Pattle: Do you have any regrets about how the expedition went?

[Moor looks at the sky, which is notably, terrifyingly dark for this time of day. He narrows his eyes.]

Moor: We ran too much. We left loot. But... we are breathing. Regret is for the dead.

Elswyth Pattle: And what about the artifact? Aramil seems to have secured... something.

[Moor stops cleaning his mace. He looks toward Aramil's tent with a heavy, unblinking stare.]

Moor: Aramil found a shiny thing. He is happy. I hope the shiny thing does not bite.

Closing

[Moor stands up, his joints popping audibly. He places the clean mace back in its loop. He pats Elswyth gently on the head with a massive hand, almost knocking her over, and walks away toward the fire without another word.]

Interview: Lavender Villani

Tiefling Warlock (Genie/Chain) | Interviewed inside her luxurious Genie Vessel (a ring)

Setting

[The interior of the ring is opulent—pillows, silks, and the smell of expensive incense. Lavender is lounging on a velvet chaise, sipping a glass of wine that definitely shouldn't exist in a jungle. Her imp, Tika, is currently a raven, preening on her shoulder. She looks pristine, despite the humidity.]

Q&A

Elswyth Pattle: Miss Villani, thank you for inviting me in. It’s... much nicer in here than out there.

[Lavender laughs, a tinkling, practiced sound. She waves a hand dismissively.]

Lavender: Darling, everywhere is nicer than out there. Did you see the humidity? But yes, welcome to the VIP lounge.

Elswyth Pattle: You seemed to handle the magical traps with a certain... flair. Specifically, using the Gust of Wind to clear the gas. That was quick thinking.

[She inspects her nails, looking pleased.]

Lavender: Well, obviously. I mean, the boys were all hacking and coughing and looking dreadful. Someone had to be the thermostat. Besides, Tika told me the air was wretched. I trust Tika more than I trust anyone with a beard in this party.

Elswyth Pattle: Speaking of Tika, your familiar took quite a hit from that giant snake. He... died, didn't he?

[Lavender’s smile tightens. She reaches up and strokes the raven’s feathers with genuine tenderness. The performance drops for a split second.]

Lavender: He did. It was rude. Extremely rude of the snake. Tika is family. You don't eat family. But... we have an arrangement, Jezebel and I. He comes back. He always comes back. But I made sure that snake burned for it. Did you see the Hellish Rebuke? It matched my outfit perfectly.

Elswyth Pattle: You and Opal seem close. You were guiding her when she was blind.

[She softens visibly. She sets her wine down.]

Lavender: Oh, Opal. She’s a sweetie, isn’t she? Big as a house, heart of a... bigger house. When she couldn’t see, she looked so small. I couldn’t have that. A blind Goliath is just a tragedy waiting to happen. Plus, holding hands is nice. Keeps her from walking into laser beams.

Elswyth Pattle: You ended up immune to fire thanks to that Zephyr Reliquary you found. That required removing the room's cooling system, which woke the snake. Any regrets?

[She laughs loudly, throwing her head back.]

Lavender: Regrets? Darling, look at me. I walked through fire. I looked like a goddess. The snake was going to wake up anyway—it’s a dungeon. Everything wakes up and tries to kill you. I just made sure I looked hot—but not too hot—while doing it.

Elswyth Pattle: The sun hasn't risen. It's pitch black outside. Does that worry you?

[She glances at the "window" of her vessel. She shrugs, swirling her wine.]

Lavender: It’s a mood. Very gothic chic. I’m sure it’s fine. Aramil did something nerdy, Bob did something sneaky, and now it’s dark. We’ll fix it after a nap. Or we won’t. I have darkvision.

Closing

[Lavender drains her glass and snaps her fingers. The raven caws. She offers Elswyth a chocolate from a silver bowl, winks, and then pointedly picks up a sleeping mask, signaling the interview is over.]

Interview: Aramil

Half-Elf Warlock (Archeologist/Academic) | Interviewed behind a stack of crates

Setting

[Aramil is crouched in the shadows behind the supply crates, frantically scribbling in a leather-bound journal. He looks gaunt, his fedora is stained with sweat and soot, and he keeps checking over his shoulder. He clutches a heavy satchel to his chest. He looks like a man vibrating with caffeine and paranoia.]

Q&A

Elswyth Pattle: Professor? Or is it Mr. Aramil?

[Aramil jumps, snapping the book shut. He adjusts his glasses crookedly.]

Aramil: Adjunct Professor Emeritus! Or... just Aramil is fine for the paperwork. Just don't put "Assistant." My father reads these, you know.

Elswyth Pattle: Right. You seemed very focused on the murals and the history of the temple. Did you find what you were looking for?

Aramil: [He lowers his voice, leaning in.] I found vindication, Elswyth. This isn't just a "temple." It's a complex solar-locking mechanism! The Man and the Crocodile? It's metaphor! Symbology! The others, they just see loot. I see history. I see tenure.

Elswyth Pattle: You tried to speak to the giant constrictor snake. You called it "Brother." That was... a bold strategy.

[He grimaces, rubbing his ribs where the snake squeezed him.]

Aramil: It was a diplomatic attempt! I speak with animals! Usually they are more conversational. "Where is the nut?" "I saw a hawk." This one was... rude. Philistine. It didn't appreciate the cultural significance of not eating me. But I survived! I survived to document it.

Elswyth Pattle: Let's talk about the T-Rex. You went for the idol.

[Aramil clutches his bag tighter. His knuckles are white.]

Aramil: The idol belongs in a museum! Or... at the very least, in my study at the Royal Community College. It was hanging there. The T-Rex was a variable I calculated. I used Phantasmal Force. I distracted the rival party! It was brilliant! Did you see the raptors I conjured in their minds? Pure psychological warfare.

Elswyth Pattle: Aramil... the sun is gone. The entire jungle is in darkness. Do you think that has anything to do with the artifact you took?

[Aramil freezes. He looks at the bag. He looks at the sky. He looks at Elswyth. He starts sweating profusely.]

Aramil: Correlation does not imply causation! That is basic science! Perhaps it is... an eclipse? A very long, sudden, magical eclipse? Look, I secured the asset. The asset is safe. If the sun has decided to take a sabbatical, that is hardly my fault. Dammit Elswyth, I'm an archeologist, not a meteorologist!

Elswyth Pattle: Are you going to show Fizzleblast what you found?

[He laughs, a high-pitched, hysterical sound.]

Aramil: Ha! Show him? And let him take the credit? No, no, no. This is my discovery. Aramil's Discovery. Has a ring to it, doesn't it?

Closing

[Aramil stands up abruptly, shoving the journal into his pocket. He looks wildly around the camp, pulls his fedora down low over his eyes, and scurries away toward the latrines, clutching his satchel like it contains his own heart.]

Interview: Opal

Goliath Barbarian | Interviewed by the cooking fire

Setting

[Opal is sitting on the ground, but she is still taller than Elswyth standing up. She is currently eating a roasted lizard on a stick—the whole lizard. She looks battered, slightly singed, but beaming. She is wearing a belt that looks far too expensive for the jungle.]

Q&A

Elswyth Pattle: Opal! You look like you've been through a war.

Opal: [Mouth full] It was great! Did you see me? I was hewed! And jacked! I got this belt. It makes me feel like I could throw a house. Do you have a house? I could throw it.

Elswyth Pattle: Please don't. You spent a large portion of the expedition blind due to the light beam. That must have been terrifying.

[Opal stops chewing. Her expression crumbles into genuine sadness for a moment.]

Opal: It was bad. I don't like the dark. I like to see things so I can hit them. When I couldn't see, I felt... small. Not Goliath small, but inside small. But Lavender held my hand! She has nice hands. Soft. Not like mine.

Elswyth Pattle: You did manage to kill the giant snake while blind, though. With a greataxe.

[She lights up again, pumping a fist in the air.]

Opal: Yeah! Chopped it like cake! A snake-cake! I just listened for the screaming. Aramil screams a lot. So I swung at the screaming and—squish! No more snake.

Elswyth Pattle: You and Bob seemed to work well together. He was... climbing on you at one point?

Opal: Bob is like a backpack that talks! He is very small. I put him on my shoulders and we are like one big person. He finds the shiny things, I carry the heavy things. It is a good system. Though he is slippery. Very oily.

Elswyth Pattle: The run at the end—the T-Rex, the fireballs, the falling rocks. You held the gate open for everyone.

Opal: [Nods solemnly] Everyone leaves. No one stays behind. That is the rule. Moor says we go, we go. I am strong, so I hold the door. If I don't hold the door, who holds the door? Aramil? [She laughs loudly] No. He has noodle arms. I have the quads.

Elswyth Pattle: It's dark out now, Opal. The sun didn't come back.

Opal: Yeah. It’s weird. Maybe the sun is sleeping? Like the T-Rex? We should be quiet so we don't wake it up. But I have my belt now. If the dark comes to fight us, I will punch the dark. With reach! My tattoo lets me punch far away!

Closing

[Opal finishes the lizard in one bite, stick and all. She flexes her arm to admire her new tattoo, causing her bicep to bulge to the size of a watermelon. She offers Elswyth a charred piece of tail with a hopeful smile.]

Interview: Bob

Halfling Rogue | Interviewed while hanging upside down from a tree branch

Setting

[Bob is difficult to find. Elswyth eventually locates him some fifteen feet up a tree on the perimeter of the camp. He is hanging by his knees, wearing a cloak that seems to ripple like a bat's wing. He is counting a stack of gold coins by touch in the dark.]

Q&A

Elswyth Pattle: Bob? Can you... come down?

Bob: No. View is better up here. Also, people can't reach my pockets. What do you want?

Elswyth Pattle: I wanted to ask about the heist. You essentially looted the entire temple while everyone else was arguing.

[He pauses counting. A coin flips over his knuckles.]

Bob: Arguing takes time. Looting takes finesse. They talk about "history" and "friendship." I talk about "weight ratios" and "market value." I got the boots, I got the cloak, I got the gold. Scoreboard says Bob wins.

Elswyth Pattle: You pulled off a swap with the Scales of Justice using flasks of oil. That was incredibly risky.

Bob: [He chuckles, a dry, rasping sound] Risk is just opportunity with bad PR. I weighed the oil. I weighed the scales. Physics doesn't lie. Magic lies, people lie, physics? Physics is my buddy. Plus, now I can... twist fate a little. Feel lucky.

Elswyth Pattle: You acted as a distraction for the pack of baboons and gorillas. You ran down the hall screaming to save the others. That... didn't seem like something you'd do for money.

[Bob goes still. He flips the cloak over his face for a second, then back.]

Bob: Strategic retreat. I was... leading them into a trap. Yeah. A trap. I wasn't saving anyone. Dead party members can't carry loot for me. Opal is my pack mule. If the mule gets eaten, I have to carry my own stuff. Can't have that.

Elswyth Pattle: You flew. Actual flight.

Bob: [Voice drops an octave, very serious] I am the night. I am the wind. I am... Bob. It was cool, okay? Don't tell them I said it was cool. But jumping off that ledge? With the T-Rex screaming? Best moment of my life. Better than sex. Better than gold. Well... maybe not gold.

Elswyth Pattle: Aramil took the eye. You took the idol. Do you think you got away with it?

Bob: Fizzleblast is an idiot. He's wearing cashmere in a swamp. He didn't see anything. But... the lights went out. Like, out out. That's usually bad. But hey, I work better in the dark. Let the tall ones worry about the sun. I got mine.

Closing

[Bob tucks the coins into a pouch that vanishes into his clothing. He releases his knees and drops fifteen feet, his cloak snapping open to slow his fall silently. He lands without a sound, winks one gleaming eye at Elswyth, and melts into the shadows of the camp.]

Soundtrack

Music

Character Theme Songs - Suno AI Prompts

Campaign: Charisma Company: Fizzleblast's Fieldtrip (One-Shot)

Generated: February 26, 2026

Structure Assignment Table

Character Structure Name Reasoning
Moor F "The Crescendo" Stoic healer who starts quiet and builds to full power — one earned chorus mirrors his understated heroism finally being recognized
Aramil E "The Confession" Spoken sections are his academic posturing and private desperation; sung sections are the performance he puts on for the world
Opal K "The Anthem" Simple, powerful identity — more chorus than verse, pure physical energy, short and punchy like her decision-making
Lavender D "The Spiral" Evolving refrain mirrors her arc from "I don't know how to play D&D" to session MVP; each repetition gains new weight
Bob G "The Loop" Obsessive hook ("just here for the loot") that transforms as the verses reveal who he really is underneath the persona

Moor — "Few Words"

Concept

Moor's theme captures the tension between his radical economy of language and the enormous weight of what he carries — 148 HP of healing, every party member's life in his clawed hands, and a faith in Tyr that he expresses through action rather than prayer. The song starts as a whisper and builds to a single, devastating chorus that earns every decibel. Like Moor himself, it says more with less.

Genre-to-Archetype Rationale

  • Class/Archetype: Life Domain Cleric
  • Mapped Genre: Sacred Ambient building to Hymnal Rock
  • Why: Clerics demand spiritual weight, but Moor is not performative. He needs a sound that starts sparse and monastic — low chanting, a single bell — then builds to something undeniable. The restraint IS the character.

Song Architecture

  • Structure: F — "The Crescendo"
  • Sequence: [Intro (ambient)] -> [Verse 1 (quiet)] -> [Verse 2 (building)] -> [Pre-Chorus] -> [Chorus (full power)] -> [Interlude] -> [Final Chorus (bigger)] -> [End]
  • Why This Structure: Moor does not announce himself. He tests the pressure plates with his mace before stepping forward. The entire song builds toward one chorus — one moment where the quiet healer becomes undeniable. The delayed payoff mirrors a character who healed 148 HP without ever asking for thanks.

Suno Style Prompt

```

Sacred hymnal rock, slow build to driving tempo, pipe organ and war drums with clean electric guitar, deep male baritone vocals, cinematic production, stoic and reverent

```

Suno Lyrics

```

[Intro]

(Low drone — single bell tolling, distant)

[Verse 1]

[Whispered]

Run through door

Why use many word

When few word do trick

[Spoken]

Shield up. Mace out. I go first.

[Verse 1 continued]

White robes and dragon scales, I walk the line ahead

I test the ground before you step, I count the wounds you bled

I do not preach, I do not pray where anyone can see

The god of justice knows my hands — that is enough for me

[Verse 2]

(Drums enter, building)

They fell to gas and fire, they fell to fang and stone

I spent my last spell slot to keep them from the bone

Lesser Restoration for the one who lost her sight

Mass Cure Wounds at midnight, holding back the night

The skeletons came seven deep, I turned them all to dust

My shield is dented, mace is cracked — I Mend because I must

One hundred forty-eight, the number that I bear

Not glory, not a battle cry — just always being there

[Pre-Chorus]

[Building, urgent]

They ask me how I'm feeling

I ask how's everybody's health

The dragon scales are heavy but

I carry more than just myself

[Chorus]

[Belting, full power]

I am the first through every door

The shield that never breaks

I heal the ones who cannot stand

I bear the weight it takes

Few words — but every one I speak

Is carved in Tyr's own stone

I'll go first into the dark

So none of you walk alone

[Interlude]

(Pipe organ swell, war drums building)

[Final Chorus]

[Choir, massive]

I am the first through every door

The shield that never breaks

I heal the ones who cannot stand

I bear the weight it takes

Few words — but every one I speak

Is sworn upon my name

I'll spend my final spell for you

And I would do the same

[End]

[Whispered]

Go. Leave. Run.

I go first.

```

Generation Notes

  • Suno Version: Latest available model
  • Instrumental Toggle: Off (vocal track)
  • Duration Target: 180-210 seconds
  • Retry Strategy: If the build does not feel gradual enough, add "crescendo" and "dynamic" to Style Prompt. If the whispered intro gets lost, try starting with the pipe organ drone louder.
  • Variation Ideas:
  • Acoustic meditation version: "Ambient sacred folk, slow, hand drums and singing bowls, whispered male vocals, intimate lo-fi production, contemplative and solemn"
  • Battle version: "Aggressive hymnal metal, driving tempo, distorted organ and double bass drums, powerful male growling vocals, stadium production, righteous and furious"

Aramil — "Adjunct Professor Emeritus"

Concept

Aramil's theme is a confession he will never actually make — the private thoughts of a man who doomed a continent for a promotion. The spoken sections are his academic deflections and desperate rationalizations; the sung sections are the truth underneath. Gothic darkwave suits the warlock archetype, but Aramil's specific flavor is intellectual decay — a man who once had credentials rotting into something dangerous.

Genre-to-Archetype Rationale

  • Class/Archetype: Warlock (patron unknown)
  • Mapped Genre: Gothic Darkwave with Academic Chamber Elements
  • Why: Warlocks get darkness and pact-cost, but Aramil's pact is not with a patron — it is with his own ego. The chamber music elements (harpsichord, strings) nod to his academic pretensions; the darkwave distortion is what those pretensions have become.

Song Architecture

  • Structure: E — "The Confession"
  • Sequence: [Spoken Word Intro] -> [Verse 1] -> [Chorus] -> [Spoken Bridge] -> [Verse 2] -> [Chorus] -> [Spoken Outro]
  • Why This Structure: The spoken sections are Aramil's public face — the academic, the professor, the man who insists on his title. The sung sections are what he cannot say aloud: desperation, loneliness, and the growing horror of what he has done. The oscillation between performance and truth IS Aramil.

Suno Style Prompt

```

Gothic darkwave, mid-tempo, harpsichord and distorted synth bass, haunted male tenor vocals, atmospheric production, brooding and desperate

```

Suno Lyrics

```

[Spoken Word]

I am adjunct professor emeritus.

Thank you.

I recently took a sabbatical from my position at the Royal Community College.

...It was not voluntary.

[Verse 1]

I wear a stained fedora like a crown upon my head

I read the ancient murals that the others left unread

The serpent's eye was watching from its golden idol throne

I knew what it would cost the world — I took it for my own

The gloves made my fingers quick, the darkness made them sure

I palmed the gem while no one looked, my motives still obscure

An eighteen on the sleight of hand, nobody saw the theft

Just scholarly acquisition — nothing precious left

[Chorus]

Maybe my dad will finally notice me

Maybe the college takes me back

Maybe the sun will rise tomorrow

If I don't look at what I lack

Every artifact I'm clutching

Every title that I claim

Cannot fill the empty lecture hall

Where no one knows my name

[Spoken Bridge]

(Harpsichord, stripped)

If it gets me reinstated at the Royal Community College...

then we're okay.

...We're okay.

Brother Snake — there are tasty monkeys down there.

Brother Gorallon — think about your wife and kids.

I negotiate with everything.

Except myself.

[Verse 2]

The sun went dark behind me as we stumbled toward the shore

The consequence is permanent — no sabbatical anymore

I told the snake to eat the apes, I told myself the same

Consume the ones below you and pretend you feel no shame

They healed me when I fell to zero, carried what I dropped

I took the eye that holds the dawn and never even stopped

My father's name, my father's face, his absence is the flame

That burns the world to get a letter with my father's name

[Chorus]

Maybe my dad will finally notice me

Maybe the college takes me back

Maybe the sun will rise tomorrow

If I don't look at what I lack

Every artifact I'm clutching

Every title that I claim

Cannot fill the empty lecture hall

Where no one knows my name

[Spoken Outro]

[Whispered, fading]

The sun will not rise again in Faerun.

And I have a purple crystal necklace and a stained fedora

and an eye worth five thousand gold

and no one to show it to.

...Adjunct professor emeritus.

Thank you.

[End]

```

Generation Notes

  • Suno Version: Latest available model
  • Instrumental Toggle: Off (vocal track)
  • Duration Target: 180-210 seconds
  • Retry Strategy: If spoken sections sound awkward, reduce them to 2 lines each. If the harpsichord clashes with the darkwave, try replacing with "minor-key piano." If vocals are too clean, add "raspy" to Style Prompt.
  • Variation Ideas:
  • Acoustic guilt version: "Dark folk ballad, slow, acoustic guitar and cello, broken male vocals, raw lo-fi recording, melancholic and confessional"
  • Villainous version: "Industrial darkwave, driving tempo, heavy synth and distorted bass, sneering male vocals, aggressive production, menacing and unrepentant"

Opal — "Blind and Swinging"

Concept

Opal killed a giant constrictor snake while blind. With a critical hit. That is the entire thesis of this song. It is short, loud, and joyful — a crowd-chant anthem for a character who processes the world through muscle and enthusiasm. The song does not need subtlety because Opal does not need subtlety. Goliath feel calm or angry. This song feels angry in the best possible way.

Genre-to-Archetype Rationale

  • Class/Archetype: Barbarian
  • Mapped Genre: Tribal War-Drum Stomp Rock
  • Why: Barbarians get primal rage and war drums. Opal's specific flavor is JOY in combat — she is not angry, she is having the time of her life. The stomp-rock energy keeps the rage fun rather than dark, matching her enthusiasm for everything from dinosaurs to obsidian rocks.

Song Architecture

  • Structure: K — "The Anthem"
  • Sequence: [Chorus] -> [Verse] -> [Chorus] -> [Bridge] -> [Chorus] -> [End]
  • Why This Structure: More chorus than verse because Opal is more action than thought. Opens with the hook because she walks into rooms and announces herself. Short — under two minutes. Pure energy. A fight song you could chant at a stadium.

Suno Style Prompt

```

Tribal stomp rock, fast driving tempo, war drums and heavy guitar with crowd stomps, powerful female belting vocals, raw arena production, ferocious and joyful

```

Suno Lyrics

```
[Chorus]

[Belting]

I hate mirrors! Hold this mirror!

Blind and swinging, never clearer!

Chop it like a cake but less delicately!

I am Opal! Come and get me!

Can't see the snake but I can feel it!

Bring the axe down — watch me deal it!

Critical hit with my eyes on fire!

Goliath rage will never tire!

[Verse]

(Stomping percussion)

They call me Oaf, I call it close enough

I saw a flier, packed my stuff

Hewed and jacked and kind of dumb

But I hit harder than the sum

Of every scholar in this cave

Every wizard trying to be brave

You want to solve it? Use your brain

I'll use my axe — we get the same

I lost my eyes to light and glass

But raging through I moved too fast

The snake came close, I swung it wide

Thirty damage — snake has died

[Chorus]

[Belting, crowd chant]

I hate mirrors! Hold this mirror!

Blind and swinging, never clearer!

Chop it like a cake but less delicately!

I am Opal! Come and get me!

Can't see the snake but I can feel it!

Bring the axe down — watch me deal it!

Critical hit with my eyes on fire!

Goliath rage will never tire!

[Bridge]

[Quieter, genuine]

My sister Pearl is back in Barovia

Living happy, living free

Goliath feel calm or angry

And right now I feel both in me

I didn't get my rock

But I got something more

I got a blindfold from Lavender

And friends worth fighting for

[Chorus]

[Full power, all instruments]

I hate mirrors! Hold this mirror!

Blind and swinging, never clearer!

Chop it like a cake but less delicately!

I am Opal! Come and get me!

Can't see the snake but I can feel it!

Bring the axe down — watch me deal it!

GOLIATH RAGE WILL NEVER TIRE!
```

  • Acoustic campfire version: "Acoustic folk stomp, mid-tempo, hand drums and acoustic guitar, warm female vocals, campfire singalong energy, heartfelt and rowdy"

Lavender — "The One Who Stayed"

Concept

Lavender's theme captures her arc from self-deprecating newcomer ("I don't know how to play D&D") to the session's undeniable MVP. The spiral structure uses a short refrain — "I stayed" — that changes meaning with each repetition: first it is about staying in the fight, then staying for her friends, then staying true to herself. Her warmth, sarcasm, and quiet competence all live in a song that keeps evolving, just like she did.

Genre-to-Archetype Rationale

  • Class/Archetype: Genie Warlock, Pact of the Chain
  • Mapped Genre: Ethereal Indie Pop with Darkwave Edges
  • Why: Warlocks typically get gothic darkness, but Lavender is too warm for pure gothic. She needs something that sparkles — the genie connection, the jeweled horns, the bright fashion — with just enough shadow underneath (the pact, the cost, the fire immunity that came from removing a temple's protection). Ethereal indie pop with darkwave edges captures that duality.

Song Architecture

  • Structure: D — "The Spiral"
  • Sequence: [Verse 1] -> [Refrain] -> [Verse 2] -> [Refrain (variation)] -> [Verse 3] -> [Refrain (final form)] -> [Outro]
  • Why This Structure: Lavender's identity evolves throughout the session. The refrain "I stayed" starts as a simple statement, gains emotional weight as we learn what she stayed FOR, and transforms into something powerful by the end. No big chorus — just a truth that keeps getting truer. Like Lavender, the song earns its power quietly.

Suno Style Prompt

```

Ethereal indie pop with darkwave undertones, mid-tempo, synth pads and clean electric guitar with subtle strings, warm female alto vocals, polished intimate production, wistful and defiant

```

Suno Lyrics

```

[Verse 1]

I picked up a locket and a genie said hello

Made a deal for power that I barely learned to throw

They handed me a crossbow and I dropped it for the space

Bracers on my wrists now and some jewels upon my face

I don't know what I'm doing but I'm doing it out loud

Tika's scouting ceilings while I'm figuring the crowd

The gas came through the hallway and I found the gust of wind

Sorry I didn't do that before we all got thinned

[Refrain]

[Soft, steady]

I stayed

When the fire couldn't touch me

I stayed

And I walked right through the flame

[Verse 2]

I gave the staff to Moor because his hands could use it best

I gave the shield to Opal and a bandana for her rest

I held her hand through darkness while she couldn't see my face

If the men find out we shapeshift they will tell the church the case

The Rod of the Pact Keeper and a reliquary cold

Jezebel said take it so I took what I was told

Two spell slots and an imp who talks like Jersey in the rain

I lost him to the serpent's bite and called him back again

[Refrain]

[Building, layered]

I stayed

When the mirror beams were burning

I stayed

And I didn't look away

[Verse 3]

They scored me eight point four and called me valuable and true

But value is not numbers, it's the bandana that I drew

For Opal when her eyes were gone, for Moor when he was spent

For every ward and gust of wind and everywhere I went

I didn't grow up magical, I wished upon a chain

A locket and a promise and I'll do it all again

We need to leave, we're gonna leave, I wanna go home

But I will not leave a single one of you alone

[Refrain]

[Full power, harmonized]

I stayed

When the world was growing darker

I stayed

And I'd stay a thousand times

I stayed

I stayed

I stayed

[Outro]

[Whispered]

If she says do it... I'm going to do it.

She's my butt.

[End]

```

Generation Notes

  • Suno Version: Latest available model
  • Instrumental Toggle: Off (vocal track)
  • Duration Target: 180-210 seconds
  • Retry Strategy: If the refrain does not land with enough emotional progression, try adding [Harmonized] to the second refrain and [Choir] to the final. If the darkwave undertones overwhelm the warmth, remove "darkwave undertones" and replace with "gentle reverb." If Suno ignores the dynamic build, try a two-pass Extend strategy.
  • Variation Ideas:
  • Acoustic locket version: "Intimate acoustic pop, slow, ukulele and soft piano, gentle female vocals, bedroom recording warmth, tender and bittersweet"
  • Power version: "Anthemic synthwave, driving tempo, layered synths and electric guitar, powerful female vocals, arena production, triumphant and defiant"

Bob — "Just Bob"

Concept

Bob's theme is built on a lie he tells himself. The hook — "I'm just here for the loot" — repeats throughout the song, but each verse reveals actions that contradict it: finding gear for Opal, running screaming to draw enemies away, hanging upside-down over a sleeping T-Rex for the team. By the final transformed hook, the words are the same but the meaning has completely inverted. He is not just here for the loot. He never was. He is just Bob — and just Bob is exactly who they needed.

Genre-to-Archetype Rationale

  • Class/Archetype: Thief Rogue
  • Mapped Genre: Smoky Jazz Noir with Heist-Film Energy
  • Why: Rogues get shadows and deception. Bob's specific flavor is the heist movie protagonist — sarcastic, competent, and hiding genuine loyalty behind a mercenary front. Jazz noir gives him the cool, the swagger, and the muted trumpet that says "I'm professionals" while doing something deeply unprofessional like hanging over a dinosaur.

Song Architecture

  • Structure: G — "The Loop"
  • Sequence: [Hook] -> [Verse 1] -> [Hook] -> [Verse 2] -> [Hook] -> [Break (stripped)] -> [Hook (transformed)] -> [End]
  • Why This Structure: The repeating hook is Bob's persona — "just here for the loot" — said so often it becomes a mantra. But each verse adds context that erodes the claim. By the break, the mask slips. By the final hook, the same words mean the opposite. The loop structure IS the gap between what Bob says and what Bob does.

```

Smoky jazz noir, mid-tempo with swagger, upright bass and muted trumpet with brushed snare, cool raspy male vocals, vintage recording quality, sly and dangerous

```

```

[Hook]

I'm just here for the loot, baby

Just here for the loot

Hood down low, fingers quick

Just Bob at the root

[Verse 1]

My name is Bob, not short for nothing

Parents ran out of room

Backpack full of daggers, got a

Mustache that might move

I rerolled a one into an eighteen

Halfling luck, that's me

Cloak of the Bat, I fly at midnight

Nobody's gonna see

Dirty twenty stealth, I'm in the shadows

Spider-climbing walls

How many squirtons in a gusher?

Doesn't matter — take it all

[Hook]

I'm just here for the loot, baby

Just here for the loot

Hood down low, fingers quick

Just Bob at the root

[Verse 2]

Found a belt that makes a giant jealous

Gave it to the tall one, free

Found the boots that jump you thirty-six feet

Kept those just for me

Ran down screaming through the corridor

Drew the baboons off the pack

Crawled across the ceiling like a nightmare

No one saw me circle back

Hung above a sleeping T-Rex, upside down

Palmed the idol, clean

Flew out through a Fireball and landed

Coolest thing you've ever seen

[Hook]

I'm just here for the loot, baby

Just here for the loot

Hood down low, fingers quick

Just Bob at the root

[Break]

[Quiet, just bass]

...Who pissed me off the least?

That's who I save.

[Whispered]

That's all of them.

I save all of them.

[Hook (Transformed)]

[Full band, warm]

I'm just here for the loot, baby

Just here for the loot

But the loot was never gold, was it?

The loot was you

Hood comes down, the eyes are shining

Bob is at the root

Of every heist that saved your life

I'm just here for the loot

[End]
```
